label: component of
description: Basis risk is one of the four IRRBB sub-risks — it captures the exposure from imperfect correlation between the reference rates on assets and liabilities, which is invisible to repricing gap and duration gap analysis.

label: impacts
description: Basis risk impacts NII when the reference rates on assets and liabilities diverge — even if both sides reprice at the same time, a widening spread between reference rates squeezes net interest income.

label: amplified by
description: The LIBOR-to-SOFR transition amplified basis risk as banks migrated assets and liabilities to different replacement rates at different speeds, creating new and sometimes unexpected basis exposures.

label: component of
description: The curvature risk charge is one of the three components of the FRTB Sensitivity-Based Method — it captures second-order price sensitivity for options and structured products that is not covered by the linear delta charge.

label: quantifies regulatory cost of
description: The curvature risk charge is the regulatory capital measure for convexity risk — just as bond convexity measures the second-order price sensitivity to yield, the curvature charge measures the second-order P&L deviation from the linear delta approximation for options.

label: income metric of
description: EaR is the income-based IRRBB metric that complements EVE — it shows the near-term earnings impact of rate changes, which is what bank boards and equity analysts focus on.

label: measures risk to
description: EaR quantifies the potential decline in NII under adverse rate scenarios — it is the probabilistic wrapper around NII sensitivity that translates rate risk into an earnings impact figure.

label: analogous to
description: EaR is the banking book analogue of VaR — both express worst-case losses at a confidence level, but VaR applies to mark-to-market P&L while EaR applies to accrual-based net interest income.

label: solvency metric of
description: EVE is the present-value (solvency) lens of IRRBB — it captures how rate changes affect the economic net worth of the banking book, complementing the near-term NII view.

label: sensitive to
description: EVE is affected by repricing mismatches because assets and liabilities with different repricing dates respond differently to rate changes, creating present-value gains or losses.

label: driven by
description: The duration gap is the primary determinant of EVE sensitivity — the larger the mismatch between asset and liability durations, the more EVE changes for a given rate shock.

label: includes
description: The FRTB Sensitivity-Based Method has three components — delta, vega, and curvature charges — summed to give total SBM capital; the curvature charge captures the second-order (convexity) risk not covered by the delta sensitivity.

label: maps sensitivities to
description: The FRTB SBM requires all interest rate delta sensitivities to be mapped to the ten prescribed tenor vertices before aggregation — these vertices are the buckets used within the GIRR risk class of the sensitivity-based method.

label: complements
description: The FRTB SBM is the standardised capital charge that all banks must compute, whilst Expected Shortfall is the metric used by the Internal Models Approach — the two sit alongside each other in the FRTB framework as standardised and model-based capital floors.

label: measured via
description: EVE is one of the two core IRRBB metrics — it captures the present-value impact of rate changes on the entire banking book, providing the solvency perspective that Basel requires alongside the earnings (NII) view.

label: decomposed into
description: Repricing risk is the most fundamental component of IRRBB — the maturity and repricing mismatches between banking book assets and liabilities are the primary source of interest rate exposure.

label: hedged using
description: Interest rate swaps are the primary tool for managing IRRBB — paying or receiving fixed in a swap alters the duration profile of the banking book, allowing ALM teams to adjust the duration gap without changing the underlying loan and deposit portfolio.

label: defines buckets for
description: The ten prescribed tenor vertices (0.25Y to 30Y) are the interest rate buckets used within the FRTB SBM's GIRR risk class — sensitivities are mapped to these vertices and then aggregated using prescribed correlations to produce the IR delta capital charge.

label: receives sensitivity from
description: Under FRTB, each position's DV01 (dollar value of a basis point) is assigned to the nearest prescribed tenor vertex — the vertex then accumulates the net DV01 from all positions, which becomes the sensitivity input s_k in the capital aggregation formula.

label: adds duration to
description: Government bonds held in the HQLA buffer add duration to the asset side of the balance sheet, increasing EVE sensitivity to rate rises — the 2022 UK gilt crisis showed how LCR buffers can themselves become a source of mark-to-market loss.

label: numerator of
description: The HQLA stock — classified into Level 1, 2A, and 2B with haircuts and caps — forms the numerator of the LCR calculation, determining how much of the bank's liquid assets count toward the regulatory minimum.

label: mitigates
description: The liquidity buffer is the bank's primary defence against short-term funding stress — its composition and size determine whether the bank can meet obligations without fire-selling illiquid assets.

label: constrains
description: The LCR requirement forces banks to hold large HQLA buffers that add duration to the asset side, creating an interest rate position that ALM must manage alongside the rest of the banking book.

label: floors run-off of
description: Basel's LCR assigns prescribed run-off rates to different deposit categories, establishing a regulatory floor for how stable deposits can be assumed to be — this interacts with the behavioural assumptions used in NMD modelling.

label: mitigates
description: The LCR is the primary regulatory tool for mitigating short-term liquidity risk — by requiring banks to hold sufficient HQLA to cover 30 days of stressed outflows, it ensures a minimum buffer against funding crises.

label: complements
description: The NSFR addresses structural funding stability over a 1-year horizon, complementing the LCR's 30-day stress focus — together they form Basel III's twin liquidity standards.

label: constrains funding mix of
description: The NSFR forces ALM teams to maintain a minimum proportion of stable funding, constraining the maturity transformation strategy and often requiring more expensive long-term debt issuance or retail deposit gathering.

label: mitigates structural
description: The NSFR mitigates structural liquidity risk by requiring stable funding for illiquid assets, preventing the maturity mismatch that caused failures like Northern Rock where long-term mortgages were funded by overnight wholesale markets.

label: results from failing
description: A risk factor is classified as non-modellable precisely when it fails the Risk Factor Eligibility Test — the NMRF designation is the direct consequence of having fewer than 24 real price observations or a gap exceeding 90 days in the prior 12 months.

label: charged via stressed
description: NMRFs receive a separate stressed Expected Shortfall capital charge computed from a single worst-case scenario rather than from historical simulation — this stressed ES is typically far larger than the equivalent IMA charge, reflecting the lack of observable market data.

label: distorts
description: Optionality risk distorts EVE calculations because embedded options create non-linear rate sensitivity — mortgage prepayment shortens asset duration when rates fall, reducing the EVE gain the bank would otherwise receive.

label: component of
description: Optionality risk is one of the four IRRBB sub-risks — it captures the asymmetric exposure from embedded options in banking products that customers exercise adversely for the bank.

label: driven by
description: Non-maturity deposits are a key source of optionality risk — depositors' behavioural option to withdraw creates an embedded option that shortens liability duration when rates rise.

label: impacts
description: Repricing mismatches also affect EVE because assets and liabilities with different repricing dates have different present-value sensitivities to rate changes.

label: component of
description: Repricing risk is the most fundamental of the four IRRBB sub-risks (alongside yield curve, basis, and optionality risk) — it captures the core maturity mismatch exposure in the banking book.

label: impacts
description: Repricing risk directly impacts NII — when rate-sensitive assets and liabilities reprice at different times, rate movements create gaps that cause NII to rise or fall.

label: feeds into
description: Modellable risk factors that pass the RFET are included in the Internal Models Approach ES calculation, whilst those that fail are capitalised separately as NMRFs — the RFET therefore determines which risk factors enter the IMA versus which generate the NMRF add-on alongside the SBM floor.

label: classifies
description: The RFET is the gateway test that determines whether a risk factor is modellable or non-modellable — failing the minimum 24-observation threshold triggers NMRF classification and a separate, more punitive capital treatment.

label: component of
description: Yield curve risk is one of the four IRRBB sub-risks — it captures the exposure to non-parallel rate movements that a simple duration gap (parallel shift) analysis would miss.

label: measured at
description: Yield curve risk is measured by computing PV01 sensitivities at each tenor vertex and applying non-parallel shocks — the tenor vertices are the grid points at which curve reshaping is assessed.

label: risk from reshaping of
description: Yield curve risk is the exposure to non-parallel changes in the yield curve — steepening, flattening, and twisting — that affect different tenors of the banking book differently.

label: quantifies error of
description: Big-O notation is the standard way to express the truncation error of a Taylor expansion — writing the remainder as O(h^3) after a quadratic approximation tells practitioners exactly how fast the error shrinks as the step size decreases.

label: characterises error of
description: The Euler-Maruyama scheme has O(sqrt(Δt)) strong convergence and O(Δt) weak convergence — big-O notation is the formal language used to state these rates and to compare numerical SDE discretisation schemes.

label: computes
description: Every Greek is a partial derivative of the option price — computing how Delta changes with the underlying (Gamma) or how a multi-leg portfolio s risk decomposes requires repeated application of the chain rule.

label: extended by
description: Ito s Lemma is the stochastic generalisation of the chain rule — it adds the correction term (1/2)sigma^2 S^2 f_{SS} dt that arises because (dW)^2 = dt, making it the essential tool for differentiating functions of stochastic processes.

label: derives
description: The quadratic formula is derived by applying completing the square to the general quadratic ax² + bx + c = 0, so the technique is the foundation of the formula.

label: used in derivation of
description: Deriving the Black-Scholes closed-form solution requires completing the square in the exponent of the lognormal density to separate the integral into N(d₁) and N(d₂) terms.

label: enables integration of
description: Evaluating Gaussian integrals requires completing the square in the exponent to reduce the integrand to a standard normal density — this is the central algebraic step in deriving probabilities from the normal distribution.

label: extends
description: The characteristic function E[e^{iuX}] is the complex-variable generalisation of the moment generating function — replacing the real argument with iu ensures the expectation always exists for fat-tailed distributions where the MGF diverges.

label: used in
description: The Heston model's semi-analytic pricing formula is derived by inverting the characteristic function of log(S_T) via numerical integration in the complex plane — complex exponentials are the core computational tool for this Fourier-based approach.

label: tested via
description: Convexity is determined by computing the second derivative f″(x) using differentiation rules — f″ ≥ 0 confirms convexity, f″ ≤ 0 confirms concavity.

label: explains
description: An option’s value is a convex function of the underlying price, which is why gamma (∂²V/∂S²) is positive for long positions — convexity is the mathematical source of the gamma P&L from large price moves.

label: required by
description: Jensen’s inequality is stated in terms of convex and concave functions — understanding convexity is a prerequisite for applying the inequality to expected values in finance.

label: enforces continuity via
description: Cubic spline construction enforces continuity of the first and second derivatives at each knot using differentiation rules, which is what produces the smooth curves essential for stable hedging.

label: constructs
description: Cubic spline interpolation is the standard method for constructing the zero rate curve from discrete market quotes, ensuring smooth rates and continuous forward rates between observed tenors.

label: includes
description: The chain rule is one of the core differentiation rules — it handles composite functions and is essential for differentiating any function where one quantity depends on another through an intermediate variable.

label: inverse of
description: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus establishes that differentiation and integration are inverse operations — differentiating the integral of a function recovers the original function, making differentiation rules the mirror image of integration.

label: inverse of
description: Integration is the reverse operation of differentiation — the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus states that the integral of a derivative recovers the original function, and every integration technique has a corresponding differentiation rule it reverses.

label: applied to
description: The derivatives of ln(x) and e^x are among the most important differentiation rules — they appear whenever continuous compounding, log-returns, or the lognormal model are differentiated.

label: generalised by
description: Partial differentiation extends single-variable differentiation to functions of several variables — the same power, product, chain, and quotient rules apply, but each derivative is taken with respect to one variable while holding the others constant.

label: foundation of
description: A Taylor series is built from repeated differentiation — the nth term is the nth derivative evaluated at the expansion point, so mastery of differentiation rules is a prerequisite for constructing any Taylor approximation.

label: applied in
description: The Greeks are partial derivatives of the option price with respect to its inputs — computing Delta, Gamma, Theta, and Vega requires direct application of the chain rule, product rule, and differentiation of the normal CDF.

label: accelerates
description: The FFT accelerates European option pricing under any model with a known characteristic function, transforming the per-strike pricing problem into a single whole-surface computation.

label: prices options under
description: The FFT prices European options under the Heston model by inverting its known characteristic function, computing prices at all strikes simultaneously in milliseconds.

label: applied in
description: Part 1 of the FTC gives the key identity F_X’(x) = f_X(x): the derivative of the CDF is the PDF. This relationship is used constantly when converting between cumulative probabilities and densities in option pricing and risk measurement.

label: foundation of
description: Part 2 of the FTC is what makes a PDF useful — the probability P(a < X < b) equals the integral of the density from a to b, evaluated as the antiderivative difference F(b) − F(a).

label: prerequisite for
description: Differentiating power functions using the rule d/dx(x^n) = nx^{n-1} requires fluent manipulation of indices, including negative and fractional exponents.

label: foundation of
description: Logarithms are defined as the inverse of exponentiation; fluency with index laws is necessary to manipulate exponential and logarithmic expressions in pricing and compounding.

label: generalises
description: Surds are roots written as fractional exponents; index laws provide the rules for manipulating these fractional powers.

label: applied in
description: The Black-Scholes PDE is universal across all European options — the terminal condition (payoff) and boundary conditions are what select the unique pricing solution for each specific contract type.

label: determines
description: The terminal condition V(S,T) = max(S-K,0) for a call or max(K-S,0) for a put is the boundary condition that, combined with the Black-Scholes PDE, yields the closed-form European option price.

label: uses
description: The integrating factor method relies on recognising that multiplying by e^{integral P dt} makes the left-hand side an exact derivative, which can then be integrated directly using standard rules.

label: uses
description: The integrating factor is always an exponential function e^{integral P(t) dt}, making properties of the exponential and logarithm essential to the technique.

label: does not solve
description: Unlike the Vasicek model, the CIR model has state-dependent diffusion sigma*sqrt(r) that prevents a simple integrating factor solution — more advanced techniques are needed.

label: solves
description: The integrating factor e^{gamma t} is the standard technique for solving the Vasicek/OU SDE analytically, producing the closed-form solution showing exponentially decaying memory of the initial condition.

label: applied in
description: Taylor series can be integrated term by term within their radius of convergence, and many integral representations of special functions (such as the error function used in option pricing) are derived by integrating a Taylor expansion.

label: foundation of
description: The CDF is defined as the integral of the probability density function from minus infinity to x — computing any probability from a continuous distribution is a direct application of definite integration.

label: applied in
description: A valid probability density must integrate to one over its support, and every probability calculation involves integrating the PDF over an interval — substitution and by-parts are routinely needed for non-trivial densities.

label: used in
description: The Taylor expansion is itself a lemma — a proven result invoked as a building block inside larger proofs, such as deriving the Greeks or the Itô correction term.

label: proves
description: The variance addition lemma for independent random variables — Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) — is the foundational step behind the square-root rule for scaling volatility.

label: exemplified by
description: Itô's Lemma is the most important lemma in quantitative finance — a proven stepping-stone result that is invoked every time a function of a stochastic process needs to be differentiated.

label: stronger than
description: Little-o is a strictly stronger statement than big-O: f = o(g) implies f = O(g) but not vice versa — understanding both notations is necessary to distinguish terms that are merely bounded (big-O) from terms that genuinely vanish (little-o) in asymptotic arguments.

label: used in derivation of
description: The derivation of Ito's lemma discards terms that are o(dt) — such as (ΔS)^2 Δt and higher powers of Δt — and retains only those of order dt, making little-o notation the formal justification for the simplifications that produce the final stochastic differential.

label: extends
description: Continuous compounding A = Pe^{rt} is the limiting case of discrete compounding — the exponential function provides the natural framework for discounting and present value calculations in continuous time.

label: foundation of
description: The lognormal distribution arises because if ln(S) is normally distributed then S = e^X is lognormal — the entire model rests on the inverse relationship between log and exponential functions.

label: defines
description: Log-returns r_t = ln(S_t/S_{t-1}) are the standard return measure in quantitative finance because the logarithm converts multiplicative price ratios into additive quantities that are easier to model statistically.

label: requires
description: Newton-Raphson requires the derivative of the function at each iteration — for implied volatility this is vega, computed via differentiation of the Black-Scholes formula.

label: uses vega from
description: The Newton-Raphson implied volatility solver uses vega (the option price sensitivity to volatility) as the derivative in each iteration step, making vega essential to the algorithm s convergence.

label: solves for
description: Newton-Raphson is the standard algorithm for computing implied volatility — it iteratively inverts the Black-Scholes formula using vega as the derivative to converge on the volatility matching the observed option price.

label: classifies
description: The Black-Scholes PDE is a parabolic partial differential equation — its discriminant B^2 - 4AC = 0 because there is no u_yy term. The parabolic classification guarantees that option prices are smooth even when payoffs have kinks.

label: classifies
description: The Fokker-Planck (Kolmogorov forward) equation is a parabolic PDE — it describes the time evolution of transition probability densities for diffusion processes, and its parabolic nature ensures these densities smooth out over time.

label: prerequisite for
description: Initial and boundary conditions constrain solutions to PDEs, which are equations expressed in terms of partial derivatives — understanding partial differentiation is essential before specifying or interpreting these constraints.

label: foundation of
description: Parabolic PDEs are second-order partial differential equations — classifying and solving them requires fluency in partial derivatives, including the second-order terms u_xx, u_xy, and u_yy that enter the discriminant.

label: used in
description: The similarity solution method relies on computing partial derivatives of the ansatz p = t^a f(xi) with respect to both t and y using the chain rule for composite functions — partial differentiation is the mechanical core of the technique.

label: foundation of
description: The Black-Scholes PDE is expressed entirely in terms of partial derivatives of the option price with respect to the underlying price, time, and volatility — without partial differentiation the equation cannot be stated or solved.

label: applied in
description: Each Greek is a partial derivative of the option price: Delta = dV/dS, Gamma = d2V/dS2, Theta = dV/dt, Vega = dV/dsigma — computing and interpreting them requires fluency in partial differentiation.

label: derived via
description: The quadratic formula is derived by completing the square on the general quadratic ax² + bx + c = 0.

label: produces
description: The quadratic formula yields roots containing √(b²−4ac), which are surds whenever the discriminant is not a perfect square.

label: used in
description: Finding eigenvalues of a 2×2 matrix requires solving the characteristic equation, which is a quadratic — the quadratic formula gives the eigenvalues directly.

label: relies on
description: Rationalisation uses index laws (especially a^{1/2} × a^{1/2} = a) to eliminate roots — the difference-of-squares identity is an application of exponent arithmetic.

label: applies to
description: Rationalisation is the primary technique for eliminating surds from denominators, making it the direct practical companion to surd theory.

label: simplifies
description: Rationalising expressions containing σ√T is a routine step when deriving and simplifying Black-Scholes Greeks and closed-form option pricing results.

label: technique for
description: The exponential substitution u = e^{αx+βt} v reduces any constant-coefficient linear parabolic PDE to the heat equation — exploiting the fact that the heat equation has known analytical solutions.

label: complements
description: Once reduced to the heat equation, the solution is a convolution with the Gaussian kernel — the same self-similar structure studied in similarity solutions, connecting the substitution technique to the fundamental solution.

label: solves
description: The Black–Scholes PDE is reduced to the heat equation via a change of variables and exponential substitution — this is how the closed-form Black–Scholes formula is derived, producing the N(d₁) and N(d₂) terms.

label: relies on
description: Richardson extrapolation requires knowing the order p of the leading error term — expressed using big-O notation as O(h^p) — so that the cancellation step uses the correct weighting factor 2^p.

label: accelerates
description: Richardson extrapolation can be applied to a finite-difference PDE solver by running the grid at two resolutions and combining the results, cancelling the leading error term and achieving fourth-order accuracy from a second-order scheme at little extra cost.

label: derives
description: The similarity exponent b = 1/2 that emerges from dimensional consistency is exactly the square-root-of-time scaling — volatility scales as sigma*sqrt(T) because the underlying diffusion equation is self-similar with this exponent.

label: solves
description: Applying the similarity ansatz to the Fokker-Planck equation for free Brownian motion yields the Gaussian transition density — the fundamental solution that underpins all diffusion-based pricing models.

label: generalised by
description: Surds are roots expressed as fractional exponents (a^{1/n}), and logarithmic and exponential functions generalise this relationship — understanding surd manipulation is the algebraic prerequisite for working fluently with exponential and log expressions in pricing formulae.

label: appears in
description: The Black-Scholes formula contains the term σ√T in the definitions of d₁ and d₂ — simplifying and manipulating this surd expression correctly is essential when computing option prices and sensitivities by hand.

label: foundation of
description: The square-root rule for scaling volatility (σ_annual = σ_daily × √252) is a direct application of surd arithmetic — understanding how to simplify and combine square roots is necessary to apply the rule correctly across different time horizons.

label: applied in
description: The continuous-time limit derivation uses a Taylor expansion of the option value in powers of delta-t to extract the Black-Scholes PDE from the discrete binomial pricing formula.

label: foundation of
description: The Delta-Gamma approximation is a second-order Taylor expansion of the option price around the current underlying price — it directly applies the Taylor series truncated at the quadratic term.

label: motivates
description: Itô’s Lemma is the stochastic calculus equivalent of a Taylor expansion — it tells you how a function of a random process changes, using the same first- and second-derivative structure. The proof of Itô’s Lemma proceeds by Taylor-expanding f(S, t) to second order and substituting (dW)² = dt — the Taylor expansion is not merely motivational but the actual derivation technique.

label: implements
description: This card translates the closed-form Black-Scholes equation into vectorised Python code — the mathematical formula for d₁, d₂, and the call/put prices maps directly to NumPy and SciPy operations, making the implementation a line-by-line mirror of the theory.

label: extends to
description: The vectorised Black-Scholes implementation naturally extends to computing Greeks — delta is computed analytically via N(d₁), and other Greeks follow from the same d₁/d₂ building blocks or by finite difference on the pricing function.

label: computes
description: The Python implementation computes delta as norm.cdf(d₁) for calls and norm.cdf(d₁) − 1 for puts — the Example section demonstrates this across a grid of spot prices, showing how delta varies from deep out-of-the-money to deep in-the-money.

label: implements
description: This card implements the Monte Carlo simulation concept in Python using NumPy's vectorised operations — the theory of averaging random payoffs is the same, but the implementation replaces path-by-path loops with matrix algebra for orders-of-magnitude speedup.

label: simulates
description: The Python simulation discretises the GBM SDE into log-normal increments — each column of the random matrix is one realisation of the geometric Brownian motion path from t=0 to T.

label: applies
description: The vectorised time-stepping scheme is the Euler-Maruyama discretisation applied to the log-price process — the exp(·) wrapper converts the additive increments back to multiplicative GBM steps.

label: accelerates
description: Monte Carlo simulation generates thousands or millions of price paths and aggregates statistics across them — vectorised NumPy operations replace per-path Python loops, making the difference between a simulation that runs in seconds and one that takes hours.

label: enables efficient computation of
description: Computing means, variances, skewness, and kurtosis across large return series is a natural array operation — NumPy vectorisation turns what would be slow iterative summations into single-call aggregations over the entire dataset.

label: implements
description: Simulating GBM paths requires generating correlated normal increments and computing cumulative products — vectorised NumPy expresses the entire path generation as array operations on random draws, avoiding per-step Python loops.

label: calibrates
description: SciPy root-finding (brentq) is the standard numerical method for inverting the Black-Scholes formula to extract implied volatility from observed option prices — the most common calibration task in derivatives.

label: computes
description: Implied volatility cannot be solved analytically from the Black-Scholes formula — SciPy root-finding algorithms (brentq, newton) numerically invert the pricing equation to recover the market-implied vol from each observed option price.

label: fits
description: Calibrating a stochastic volatility model (Heston, SABR) to the observed volatility surface requires minimising the pricing error across many strikes and expiries simultaneously — SciPy minimize orchestrates this multi-parameter optimisation.

label: implements
description: The pandas ewm() method implements exponentially weighted moving average calculations, including EWMA volatility — the same lambda-weighted variance estimator used in RiskMetrics and other risk models.

label: implements analysis of
description: pandas provides the vectorised methods (pct_change, rolling, resample) used to compute and analyse the statistical properties of returns — mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis, and autocorrelation — directly from price series.

label: computes
description: pandas rolling().std() computes the standard deviation over a moving window — the same variance and standard deviation concepts applied incrementally across a time series to produce rolling volatility estimates.

label: implements
description: Portfolio optimisation in Python traces the efficient frontier computationally by repeatedly solving the constrained minimisation at a grid of target returns — the numerical counterpart to Markowitz's analytical frontier.

label: implements
description: SciPy's constrained optimiser is the practical tool for implementing Markowitz mean-variance optimisation — it finds the optimal asset weights that modern portfolio theory defines analytically but which require numerical methods for real-world portfolios with many assets and constraints.

label: requires
description: The covariance matrix passed to the portfolio optimiser must be positive semi-definite for the quadratic objective to be convex and the minimum-variance solution to exist — if the sample covariance is not PSD (due to missing data or numerical issues), the optimiser may fail or produce nonsensical weights.

label: applies
description: Cholesky decomposition is used in random number generation to convert independent standard normal draws into correlated draws — multiplying by the lower-triangular Cholesky factor of the correlation matrix produces the required joint distribution.

label: generates inputs for
description: Random number generation produces the stochastic draws that drive Monte Carlo simulation — each simulated path begins with a vector of pseudorandom normal variates that are transformed into asset price movements.

label: implements numerically
description: Random number generation with Cholesky-correlated normal draws is the numerical implementation of correlated Brownian motions — each column of the transformed random matrix represents a discrete approximation to a correlated Wiener process.

label: requires
description: Pricing an American option requires evaluating the early exercise decision at every node — the option price is the maximum of the intrinsic value and the continuation value.

label: extends
description: An American option is worth at least as much as its European counterpart — the early-exercise premium is the additional value from the right to exercise before expiry.

label: compares
description: The comparison card contrasts the pricing methodologies, exercise rights, and put-call parity relationships that distinguish American from European options.

label: compares
description: The comparison card highlights that European options admit closed-form Black-Scholes prices and exact put-call parity, serving as the analytically tractable baseline.

label: constrains
description: Put-call parity holds exactly for European options but only as inequality bounds for American options — the early-exercise right breaks the strict equality.

label: alternative to
description: The Bachelier model is the normal-distribution alternative to Black-Scholes' lognormal framework — it predates Black-Scholes by 73 years and has returned to prominence for pricing options on negative interest rates.

label: prices
description: Swaption markets now routinely quote Bachelier (normal) implied volatility alongside or instead of Black implied volatility, especially for EUR and JPY rates that can be negative.

label: applies
description: The Bachelier model embodies the normal side of the normal-vs-lognormal choice — it assumes arithmetic Brownian motion (normal prices) rather than geometric (lognormal), allowing the underlying to go negative.

label: extended by
description: American option pricing uses backward induction with an additional early-exercise check at each node — the algorithm compares the continuation value against the immediate exercise payoff and keeps the maximum.

label: computes
description: At each node of the backward pass, the hedge ratio (delta) is computed as the ratio of the change in option value to the change in stock price across the two successor nodes, giving the exact number of shares needed to replicate the option.

label: prices
description: Backward induction on a binomial tree prices European options by discounting terminal payoffs step-by-step to the present — as the number of steps grows, the price converges to the Black-Scholes closed form.

label: implements
description: Each backward step applies the one-period risk-neutral pricing formula — discounting the probability-weighted average of successor values at the risk-free rate — making backward induction a discrete implementation of risk-neutral valuation.

label: priced by
description: The cash-or-nothing binary call price Q·e^(-rT)·N(d₂) is derived directly from the Black-Scholes framework — N(d₂) is the risk-neutral probability that the option expires in the money.

label: models payoff of
description: A merger arbitrage position has a binary payoff structure — a fixed profit if the deal closes, a fixed loss if it breaks — making the binary option the natural instrument for pricing and hedging deal risk.

label: illustrates
description: The binary call price equals the discounted risk-neutral probability of finishing in the money, making it the clearest illustration of the risk-neutral pricing principle: price = e^(-rT)·E^Q[payoff].

label: converges to
description: As the number of time steps N tends to infinity, the binomial tree converges to the continuous-time Black-Scholes model — this is the Cox-Ross-Rubinstein convergence result.

label: derives
description: The hedge ratio (delta) emerges naturally within the binomial framework as the ratio of the spread in option values to the spread in stock prices at each node.

label: derives
description: The binomial model derives the hedge ratio as the ratio of the option payoff spread to the stock price spread at each node — this discrete formula converges to the Black-Scholes delta in the continuous-time limit.

label: demonstrates
description: The binomial model is the simplest demonstration of risk-neutral pricing — the real-world probability cancels out and only the risk-neutral probability p-prime enters the pricing formula.

label: defines
description: The Black-Scholes formula provides the closed-form expression for Delta as N(d1) for calls and N(d1)-1 for puts, making it the standard reference for computing and interpreting Delta.

label: enables
description: Black-Scholes shows that a continuously rebalanced Delta hedge eliminates all price risk, which is precisely the argument used to derive the Black-Scholes PDE.

label: prices
description: The Black-Scholes formula gives closed-form prices for European calls and puts — it is the standard analytical pricing tool for European-style options. The closed-form Black–Scholes formula applies only to European-style options because it assumes the terminal payoff is the sole boundary condition — American options introduce a free boundary (early exercise) that the PDE cannot handle without numerical methods.

label: defines
description: The Black-Scholes formula gives the closed-form expression for Gamma as N prime(d1) / (S sigma sqrt(T)), the second derivative of the option price with respect to the underlying.

label: defines
description: The Black-Scholes formula gives closed-form expressions for Delta, Gamma, Vega, Theta and Rho — the Greeks are simply partial derivatives of the Black-Scholes price with respect to each input.

label: defines
description: The Black-Scholes formula gives the closed-form expression for Rho as KTe^{-rT}N(d2) for calls, the sensitivity of the option price to changes in the risk-free rate.

label: defines
description: The Black-Scholes formula provides the closed-form expression for Theta, and the Black-Scholes PDE directly links Theta to Gamma via the relationship Theta + 0.5 Gamma sigma^2 S^2 = rV.

label: defines
description: The Black-Scholes formula gives the closed-form expression for Vega as S N prime(d1) sqrt(T), the sensitivity of the option price to changes in implied volatility.

label: inverted to get
description: Implied volatility is found by running Black-Scholes in reverse: given a market price for an option, you solve numerically for the volatility that makes the formula match.

label: enforces
description: Cash-and-carry arbitrage is the mechanism that enforces the no-arbitrage forward price — any deviation from F* = S₀e^(rT) is immediately exploited, driving the forward price back to fair value.

label: applies
description: The fair forward price F* = S₀e^(rT) is derived directly from the time value of money — carrying the spot asset to expiry costs exactly the compounded risk-free interest on the purchase price.

label: analogous to
description: Charm and Colour are both time-decay Greeks for spot sensitivities: Charm measures how Delta drifts with time, while Colour measures how Gamma drifts with time.

label: refines
description: Charm tells a hedger how much their Delta will drift with the passage of time, enabling them to anticipate and pre-adjust hedge ratios over weekends and holidays when no rebalancing is possible.

label: assumed by
description: The Black-Scholes equation is derived under the assumption of a complete market — continuous trading in the stock and bond is sufficient to replicate any European payoff, yielding a unique price.

label: enables
description: Market completeness is the theoretical guarantee that delta hedging works: because every contingent claim can be replicated, the hedging portfolio perfectly offsets the option exposure.

label: guarantees uniqueness of
description: In a complete market the risk-neutral measure is unique — completeness plus no-arbitrage together ensure there is exactly one pricing measure, which is the second Fundamental Theorem of Asset Pricing.

label: derives
description: Taking the continuous-time limit of the binomial model produces the Black-Scholes PDE — this card shows how the discrete one-step formula converges to the continuous PDE as the time step shrinks to zero.

label: arguments of
description: d₁ and d₂ are the two arguments fed into the normal CDF in the Black-Scholes formula — understanding what they encode (log-moneyness, drift, and volatility scaling) is essential to interpreting and differentiating the formula.

label: standardises
description: d₁ and d₂ standardise the option's moneyness by dividing log(S/K) by the volatility-scaled time horizon σ√T, converting moneyness into a z-score suitable for normal CDF evaluation.

label: arguments passed to
description: d₁ and d₂ are the specific values passed to N(·) and N'(·) in the Black-Scholes formula — this notation card explains how to read the CDF and PDF symbols that appear in the pricing and Greek expressions.

label: contains
description: The σ²/2 term that distinguishes d₁ from d₂ is the volatility drag from geometric Brownian motion — it reflects the gap between the arithmetic and geometric mean returns of lognormally distributed assets.

label: extended by
description: Speed provides the third-order correction term to the Delta-Gamma approximation, improving accuracy for large moves where the quadratic approximation breaks down.

label: used in
description: The Delta-Gamma approach enables faster VaR computation for options portfolios by approximating the P&L distribution with a quadratic function of the underlying rather than full repricing.

label: differentiated to give
description: Charm is the time derivative of Delta — it measures how the hedge ratio changes purely from the passage of time, even when the underlying price remains unchanged.

label: first term of
description: Delta provides the first-order (linear) term of the approximation — it captures the directional component of the option s P&L for small moves in the underlying.

label: foundation of
description: Delta is the hedge ratio itself — delta hedging works by holding exactly Delta units of the underlying to offset the option s first-order price sensitivity, so understanding Delta is a prerequisite for constructing any delta-neutral portfolio.

label: differentiated to give
description: Gamma is the derivative of Delta with respect to the underlying price — it measures how quickly the hedge ratio changes and therefore how often a delta hedge must be rebalanced.

label: cross-derivative of
description: Vanna measures how Delta changes with implied volatility — it is the cross partial derivative linking the directional sensitivity (Delta) to the volatility dimension.

label: requires management of
description: Delta hedging a long option position creates a short gamma position — the portfolio must be rebalanced as the underlying moves, making Gamma the primary measure of hedging frequency and cost.

label: underlies
description: The Black–Scholes model derives the option price by constructing a delta-hedged portfolio that earns the risk-free rate — the no-arbitrage principle ensures the price is unique and consistent.

label: exposed to
description: Delta hedging is only perfect in a continuous-time world with zero friction and constant volatility — discrete rebalancing, transaction costs, and volatility jumps are the primary limitations that break the hedge in practice.

label: informed by
description: Theta and the cost of carry determine whether early exercise is worthwhile — when Theta decay exceeds the interest earned on the intrinsic value, continuing to hold is optimal.

label: driven by
description: The decision to exercise an American option early depends on whether the benefit of immediate exercise exceeds the remaining time value — Theta quantifies the rate at which that time value is disappearing.

label: driven by
description: The core trade-off in early exercise is whether receiving cash now and earning interest outweighs the option's remaining time value — a direct application of the time value of money.

label: valued via
description: European option prices equal the discounted expected payoff under the risk-neutral measure — this expectation is the defining calculation for European-style derivatives.

label: extends
description: Expiration P&L extends the payoff diagram by subtracting the premium paid, shifting the payoff curve down by the initial cost and revealing the break-even price.

label: depends on
description: The premium paid or received is the cost basis in the expiration P&L calculation — decomposing it into intrinsic and time value explains why the break-even differs from the strike.

label: evaluates
description: Expiration P&L on a long call equals the call's terminal payoff minus the premium, providing the simplest measure of whether buying the call was a profitable trade.

label: accuracy described by
description: The truncation error of a finite difference scheme — forward, backward, or central — is described using big-O notation: the central difference has O(Δx²) error whilst the one-sided schemes have O(Δx) error, directly informing the choice of scheme in option pricing grids.

label: used for
description: American option pricing requires finding an early-exercise boundary as part of the solution — finite difference methods handle this naturally by working backwards through the grid and applying the early-exercise condition at each node.

label: solves
description: Finite difference methods discretise the Black-Scholes PDE on a price-time grid and solve it backwards from the terminal payoff — they are the standard numerical approach when no closed-form solution exists, such as for American options.

label: derived from
description: The forward price formula is derived directly from the no-arbitrage principle — if the forward deviates from the cost-of-carry value, a riskless profit can be locked in via cash-and-carry or reverse cash-and-carry arbitrage.

label: foundation of
description: The forward price equals the risk-neutral expected spot price, making it the simplest example of risk-neutral pricing — understanding forwards builds intuition for the Q-measure approach to more complex derivatives.

label: applies
description: The forward price formula is a direct application of time value of money — it compounds the spot price at the risk-free rate to reflect the opportunity cost of capital over the contract life.

label: characterises
description: The Second Fundamental Theorem characterises market completeness: a market is complete if and only if the equivalent martingale measure is unique, guaranteeing a single no-arbitrage price for every claim.

label: formalises
description: The First Fundamental Theorem formalises the no-arbitrage principle: a market is arbitrage-free if and only if an equivalent martingale measure exists.

label: justifies
description: The FTAP provides the rigorous justification for risk-neutral pricing — the existence of ℚ (first theorem) means every derivative can be priced as a discounted ℚ-expectation.

label: requires
description: Both fundamental theorems are stated in terms of equivalent probability measures — the first requires existence of ℚ, the second links uniqueness of ℚ to market completeness.

label: differentiated to give
description: Colour is the time derivative of Gamma — it measures how Gamma drifts as time passes, just as Charm measures Delta decay and Veta measures Vega decay.

label: second term of
description: Gamma provides the second-order (quadratic) correction that accounts for the curvature of the option price, improving accuracy for larger moves where the linear Delta estimate breaks down.

label: governs cost of
description: Gamma determines how rapidly Delta shifts with the underlying, and therefore how frequently and expensively a delta hedge must be rebalanced — high Gamma means high hedging cost.

label: differentiated to give
description: Speed is the derivative of Gamma with respect to the underlying price — it measures how quickly Gamma shifts as spot moves, determining the stability of Gamma hedges.

label: trades off against
description: The Gamma-Theta trade-off is fundamental: for a delta-hedged portfolio the Black-Scholes PDE forces Theta + 0.5 Gamma sigma^2 S^2 = rV, so long Gamma positions necessarily bleed Theta and vice versa.

label: differentiated to give
description: Zomma is the derivative of Gamma with respect to implied volatility — it measures how the curvature of the option price shifts when volatility changes.

label: numerical form of
description: Greeks by finite difference are the numerical implementation of differentiation rules — central differences approximate the derivative definition, with accuracy controlled by the bump size.

label: computes
description: Finite difference bumping is the universal method for computing option Greeks — it works with any pricing model by perturbing inputs and measuring the resulting price change.

label: feeds into
description: Greeks computed by finite difference feed directly into P&L attribution — the bumped sensitivities are multiplied by actual market moves to explain the daily P&L.

label: applied in
description: The hedge ratio tells the trader how many shares to hold per option — delta hedging is the continuous rebalancing strategy that keeps this ratio current as the underlying moves.

label: uses
description: The minimum-variance hedge ratio h* = rho * sigma_S / sigma_F depends directly on the correlation between spot and futures price changes — low correlation means the hedge is less effective and the optimal ratio deviates further from one.

label: implies
description: Put-call parity is a direct consequence of the law of one price: a call plus discounted cash equals a put plus stock, so both portfolios must cost the same to prevent arbitrage.

label: underpins
description: Risk-neutral pricing derives derivative values by constructing a replicating portfolio — the law of one price guarantees the derivative's price equals the replicating portfolio's cost.

label: violated by
description: A violation of the law of one price immediately implies a riskless arbitrage — buying the cheap portfolio and selling the expensive one at identical future payoffs generates a guaranteed profit with zero net investment.

label: qualifies
description: The no-arbitrage principle assumes a frictionless market, but limits to arbitrage explain why real prices can deviate from their theoretical no-arbitrage values within bounds set by transaction costs and funding spreads.

label: constrains
description: Real-world frictions such as transaction costs, funding constraints, and short-selling restrictions limit the profitability of riskless arbitrage, allowing small mispricings to persist that pure theory would predict should vanish instantly.

label: quantifies gap between
description: The market price of risk is the numerical quantity that separates ℙ from ℚ — it measures exactly how much excess drift per unit of volatility the measure change removes.

label: normalises
description: The market price of risk normalises the risk premium by volatility — θ = (μ − r)/σ expresses the risk premium per unit of risk rather than in absolute return terms.

label: equivalent to
description: The market price of risk is numerically identical to the Sharpe ratio — both equal (μ − r)/σ, measuring excess return per unit of volatility.

label: parameterises
description: The market price of risk θ = (μ − r)/σ is the parameter that Girsanov’s theorem uses to define the change of measure — it determines how much drift is absorbed into the new Brownian motion.

label: validates
description: The martingale test is the standard diagnostic that verifies a pricing implementation correctly produces the martingale property — it checks that Eᵠ[e^{−rT}S_T] ≈ S_0 across simulated paths.

label: validates
description: The martingale test is the first quality check run on a Monte Carlo engine — before trusting any option price, the quant verifies that discounted simulated prices average to the initial price.

label: instance of
description: The martingale test is a z-test of whether the sample mean of discounted terminal prices equals S₀ — it applies the standard hypothesis testing framework with H₀: implementation is correct.

label: grounded in
description: The entire martingale pricing framework is grounded in the fundamental theorems — the first theorem ensures ℚ exists (no arbitrage), the second ensures it is unique (complete market, unique prices).

label: operationalises
description: The martingale framework operationalises risk-neutral pricing — derivative values are computed as discounted ℚ-expectations precisely because discounted prices are martingales under ℚ.

label: uses for hedging
description: The martingale representation theorem guarantees that every contingent claim can be replicated by a dynamic trading strategy — the integrand in the representation is the delta hedge used on trading desks.

label: generalises
description: Merton's jump-diffusion pricing formula generalises Black-Scholes — when the jump intensity is zero the infinite series collapses to the single n=0 term and recovers the standard Black-Scholes price exactly.

label: prices
description: Merton's formula prices European options under jump-diffusion dynamics by expressing the option value as a Poisson-weighted sum of Black-Scholes prices, each conditioned on a specific number of jumps occurring before expiry.

label: uses
description: The Poisson distribution provides the probability weights in Merton's series — each term is weighted by the probability of exactly n jumps in time T, making the Poisson PMF the structural backbone of the formula.

label: prices under
description: Merton's option pricing formula is the closed-form solution for European options when the underlying follows the Merton jump-diffusion SDE — the pricing formula is the direct consequence of the jump-diffusion dynamics.

label: justified by
description: Delta hedging constructs the riskless portfolio whose existence the no-arbitrage principle requires — without a perfect hedge, the argument that the portfolio must earn exactly r collapses.

label: implies
description: Put-call parity is a direct consequence of no-arbitrage: if the relationship between a call, a put, the stock, and a bond were violated, a trader could construct a risk-free profit from the mispricing.

label: foundation of
description: The no-arbitrage condition guarantees the existence of a risk-neutral measure under which all discounted asset prices are martingales — this is the First Fundamental Theorem of Asset Pricing.

label: forbids
description: The no-arbitrage principle states that riskless arbitrage opportunities cannot persist in an efficient market — the principle is the axiom, and riskless arbitrage is the specific violation it rules out.

label: applied in
description: Triangular arbitrage is a direct application of the no-arbitrage principle to the FX market — it enforces the law of one price by ensuring the cost of a currency is independent of the conversion path.

label: generalises
description: Numéraire change generalises beyond the standard ℙ vs ℚ dichotomy to a family of measures — each tradeable asset can serve as numéraire, yielding a different measure under which a different quantity becomes a martingale.

label: creates
description: The key property of a numéraire change is that asset prices divided by the new numéraire become martingales under the new measure — choosing the natural numéraire makes the pricing expectation tractable.

label: uses
description: Each numéraire change is effected by a Radon–Nikodým derivative equal to the ratio of the new numéraire to the old, normalised by their initial values.

label: derived via
description: The Greeks are partial derivatives of the option price; a Taylor expansion in those derivatives gives a local approximation of how the portfolio P&L changes with small moves in price, time, and volatility.

label: includes
description: Colour is the third-order Greek measuring Gamma decay — it completes the time-decay picture alongside Charm (Delta decay) and Veta (Vega decay).

label: includes
description: Delta is the most fundamental of the option Greeks — it is the first partial derivative of the option price with respect to the underlying, and the starting point from which higher-order Greeks like Gamma are defined.

label: parameterises
description: Delta is the hedge ratio — it tells you exactly how many units of the underlying to hold in order to offset the option's directional exposure. Gamma measures how fast Delta changes with the underlying price — a high-Gamma position requires more frequent rebalancing of the delta hedge, making Gamma the key Greek for monitoring hedging cost and exposure between rebalances.

label: includes
description: Gamma is the second-order Greek in the Greeks family — it captures the curvature of the option price function that the first-order Delta approximation misses.

label: quantifies
description: Delta is the continuous-time hedge ratio — the option Greeks framework provides the exact number of underlying units needed to neutralise first-order price risk, which is the definition of the hedge ratio.

label: includes
description: Rho is the interest-rate sensitivity Greek — it completes the set of five first-order Greeks (Delta, Gamma, Theta, Vega, Rho) that describe an option s risk profile.

label: includes
description: Speed is the third-order spot Greek — it extends the Delta-Gamma-Speed hierarchy that describes the option price s Taylor expansion in the underlying to third order.

label: includes
description: Theta is the time-decay Greek — it measures how much option value erodes each day purely from the passage of time, and is a core component of any Greeks risk report.

label: includes
description: Vanna is the cross Greek linking Delta and Vega — it captures the interaction between directional and volatility risk that first-order Greeks miss.

label: includes
description: Vega is the volatility-sensitivity Greek — it measures how much an option s price changes per unit change in implied volatility, making it essential for volatility trading strategies.

label: includes
description: Veta is the Vega-time cross Greek — it measures the rate of Vega decay and is essential for managing calendar spreads where differential Vega decay drives profitability.

label: includes
description: Volga is the second-order volatility Greek — it measures the convexity of the option price with respect to implied volatility, extending the first-order sensitivity captured by Vega.

label: includes
description: Zomma is the cross Greek linking Gamma to volatility — it captures how the option s curvature shifts during volatility regime changes.

label: feeds into
description: In the Delta-Gamma VaR method, the option Greeks are used to approximate the portfolio loss as a polynomial of risk factor changes, allowing for rapid risk calculation without full revaluation.

label: determines behaviour of
description: Moneyness is the primary driver of an option's Greek profile — deep ITM options have delta near ±1 and minimal gamma, while ATM options have the highest gamma, theta, and vega exposure.

label: determines split of
description: Moneyness directly determines how the option premium splits between intrinsic and extrinsic value — ITM options carry both, while OTM options are entirely extrinsic.

label: classifies
description: Moneyness classifies a call as ITM, ATM, or OTM depending on whether the underlying exceeds, equals, or falls below the strike — this classification determines the call's intrinsic value and delta.

label: classifies
description: Moneyness classifies a put as ITM, ATM, or OTM depending on whether the underlying is below, at, or above the strike — this classification determines the put's intrinsic value and delta.

label: visualises
description: The protective put payoff diagram is constructed by vertically summing the long stock and long put payoff lines, revealing the characteristic kinked profile with a floor at the strike minus the premium.

label: visualises
description: The call payoff diagram plots the hockey-stick function max(S_T - K, 0), making the asymmetric risk-reward of a long call immediately visible.

label: visualises
description: The put payoff diagram plots the hockey-stick function max(K - S_T, 0), showing that the put holder profits as the underlying falls below the strike.

label: explains erosion of
description: Time decay is the mechanism by which the extrinsic (time value) component of the premium decomposition erodes to zero at expiry — understanding the split is prerequisite to understanding theta.

label: decomposes
description: The call premium decomposes into intrinsic value max(S - K, 0) plus extrinsic value — this split explains why an ITM call is worth more than its exercise value before expiry.

label: decomposes
description: The put premium decomposes into intrinsic value max(K - S, 0) plus extrinsic value — for American puts, the early exercise decision hinges on whether the interest on intrinsic value exceeds the extrinsic value lost.

label: explains context of
description: Risk-neutral pricing uses the Q measure exclusively — this card explains why the drift changes from μ to r and why the Black–Scholes price does not depend on the real-world expected return.

label: concrete instance of
description: The physical measure ℙ and risk-neutral measure ℚ are the canonical example of equivalent probability measures in finance — they agree on possible events but assign different probabilities.

label: effected by
description: Girsanov’s theorem is the mathematical tool that transforms ℙ into ℚ — it absorbs the excess drift (μ − r) into a new Brownian motion, changing the stock’s drift from μ to r.

label: analysed via
description: A protective put's expiration P&L — the sum of the stock return and the put's P&L — shows the capped downside and reduced upside that define the strategy's risk-reward profile.

label: equivalent via
description: Put-call parity shows that a protective put (long stock + long put) is economically equivalent to a long call plus a risk-free bond — S + P = C + Ke^{-rT}.

label: uses
description: The protective put strategy requires purchasing a put option whose value determines the cost of the downside protection — the put premium is the explicit price of portfolio insurance.

label: embeds
description: Put-call parity embeds the forward price — rewriting the parity relation shows that the difference between call and put prices equals the present value of the forward price minus the strike.

label: guaranteed by
description: Put-call parity is enforced by arbitrage — if the parity relationship is violated, a riskless profit can be made by simultaneously trading the option and its replicating portfolio.

label: constrains
description: Put-call parity forces the Delta of a put and the Delta of the equivalent call to sum to one (or negative one), providing a quick sanity check on any Greeks calculation.

label: constrains
description: Put-call parity forces the difference between call Rho and put Rho to equal KTe^{-rT}, providing a consistency check on any Rho calculation.

label: core of
description: The binomial model prices options by finding the replicating portfolio (Δ shares + B bonds) that matches the option's payoffs in the up and down states, then setting the option price equal to the portfolio's cost.

label: possible in
description: A replicating portfolio can be constructed for every derivative only in a complete market — completeness is precisely the condition that guarantees every payoff can be replicated by traded assets.

label: same construction as
description: Delta hedging and replication are two perspectives on the same portfolio — the delta-hedged position replicates the derivative's risk profile by holding exactly Δ shares per option.

label: constrained by
description: Risk arbitrage is the archetypal case where limits to arbitrage apply — deal risk, funding costs, and the threat of a sudden gap-down if a merger breaks prevent arbitrageurs from closing the spread to zero.

label: contrasts with
description: Unlike riskless arbitrage, which locks in a guaranteed profit with no net investment, risk arbitrage bears deal risk — the merger spread is compensation for the possibility that the transaction fails to complete.

label: priced via
description: The fair value of a merger arbitrage position is the probability-weighted payoff across deal-success and deal-failure scenarios, making expected value the central calculation for sizing and pricing the trade.

label: framework for
description: The Black-Scholes price equals the discounted expected payoff under the risk-neutral measure — risk-neutral pricing is the theoretical foundation that justifies the formula.

label: foundation of
description: Risk-neutral pricing provides the one-step discounted expectation formula that is the starting point for the continuous-time limit derivation; the risk-neutral probability is what causes the drift to cancel.

label: normalised as
description: Dividing the risk premium by volatility gives the market price of risk θ = (μ − r)/σ, which is the drift-per-unit-volatility removed by the Girsanov measure change.

label: explains gap between
description: The risk premium μ − r is the drift difference between ℙ and ℚ — it is the compensation investors require for risk, which is removed when moving to the pricing measure.

label: decomposed via
description: The CAPM decomposes an asset’s risk premium as β times the market risk premium — beta estimation provides the sensitivity coefficient that scales the systematic risk compensation.

label: example of
description: Cash-and-carry is the canonical example of a riskless arbitrage in forward markets — buying spot and selling the overpriced forward locks in a guaranteed profit with no net risk.

label: ruled out by
description: Riskless arbitrage is the exact opportunity the no-arbitrage principle asserts cannot persist in an efficient market — every derivative pricing model is built on the assumption that no such strategy exists.

label: exploits violation of
description: A breach of put-call parity is the textbook riskless arbitrage: buying the underpriced combination and shorting the overpriced one locks in a guaranteed profit with zero net investment.

label: precondition for
description: Risk-neutral pricing is mathematically valid only in an arbitrage-free market — the existence of a risk-neutral measure is equivalent to the assumption that no riskless arbitrage opportunities exist.

label: implemented via
description: Backward induction in a binomial tree — comparing exercise value with continuation value at each node — is precisely the construction of the Snell envelope in discrete time.

label: extends
description: European pricing computes a single discounted ℚ-expectation at maturity; the Snell envelope extends this to American options by taking the supremum over all stopping times.

label: relies on
description: The backward induction that constructs the Snell envelope uses the tower property at each step — the continuation value Eᵠ[U_{t+1} | ℱ_t] nests expectations consistently across time steps.

label: contrasts with
description: Risk arbitrage is driven by a specific corporate event (a merger), whereas statistical arbitrage is driven by statistical patterns in price relationships with no single fundamental catalyst.

label: contrasts with
description: Statistical arbitrage bets on mean reversion using statistical models but is not risk-free — unlike riskless arbitrage, the spread can fail to revert if the historical relationship breaks down.

label: exploits
description: Statistical arbitrage strategies depend on the autocorrelation structure of spread returns — a stationary, mean-reverting spread has negative autocorrelation that makes deviations predictably self-correcting.

label: key input to
description: The strike price K is one of the five inputs to the Black-Scholes formula, appearing in the discounted exercise cost term Ke^{-rT} and in the d1 and d2 expressions that determine option value.

label: determines break-even of
description: The strike price sets the kink point in every expiration P&L diagram — the break-even price equals the strike plus (for calls) or minus (for puts) the premium paid.

label: defines
description: Moneyness is defined entirely by the relationship between the underlying price S and the strike price K — whether an option is ITM, ATM, or OTM depends on which side of K the spot price sits.

label: compared with
description: The strike price and underlying price are the two values whose comparison determines an option's intrinsic value and moneyness — the payoff at expiration is simply the difference max(S-K, 0) or max(K-S, 0).

label: priced via Black variant of
description: Swaptions are priced using the Black (1976) model, a variant of Black-Scholes adapted for forward rates, where the annuity factor replaces the discount factor as the numeraire.

label: option on
description: A swaption is an option to enter into an interest rate swap — the underlying instrument is the swap itself, and the swaption s value depends on the forward swap rate and its volatility.

label: quoted in
description: Swaptions are quoted in terms of implied volatility (either normal or lognormal) rather than price, forming the swaption volatility cube that is the rates market s equivalent of the equity vol surface.

label: smile modelled by
description: The swaption volatility smile across strikes is modelled by SABR — each expiry-tenor point in the swaption cube has its own calibrated SABR parameters.

label: computed by
description: Black-Scholes provides the most widely used closed-form expression for the theoretical value of European options, turning the general risk-neutral pricing principle into a concrete formula.

label: derived from
description: The theoretical value of any derivative is the discounted expected payoff under the risk-neutral measure — this fundamental theorem converts the pricing problem into an expectation calculation.

label: inverted to find
description: Implied volatility is found by inverting the theoretical value formula — it is the volatility input that makes the model's theoretical value equal the observed market price.

label: related to
description: Charm and Theta are both time-related Greeks — Theta measures how the option price decays with time, while Charm measures how the hedge ratio (Delta) shifts with time.

label: analogous to
description: Theta and Veta are both time-decay measures: Theta captures how the option price decays with time, while Veta captures how Vega (volatility sensitivity) decays with time.

label: varies with
description: Time decay is strongest for ATM options and weakest for deep ITM or OTM options — moneyness determines where the option sits on the theta curve.

label: measured by
description: Theta is the Greek that quantifies time decay — it gives the rate at which the option's extrinsic value erodes per unit of time, all else equal.

label: erodes value of
description: Time decay reduces the call option's time value component as expiry approaches — the rate of erosion accelerates non-linearly, following an approximate square-root-of-time relationship.

label: erodes value of
description: Time decay reduces the put option's time value as expiry nears — for deep ITM puts, theta can occasionally be positive because the interest on the intrinsic value exceeds the time value lost.

label: is input to
description: Time to expiry T is a key input to the Black-Scholes equation — it appears in the square-root scaling of volatility and in the exponential discounting of the strike price.

label: measured by
description: Theta measures the sensitivity of an option's price to changes in time to expiry — as T shrinks, theta quantifies the rate at which time value erodes.

label: drives
description: Shrinking time to expiry is the underlying cause of time decay — as T falls, the option's time value diminishes at an accelerating rate proportional to the square root of remaining time.

label: applies
description: Option pricing uses the square-root-of-time rule because uncertainty scales as the square root of T — this is why volatility in Black-Scholes appears as sigma times the square root of T.

label: enforces
description: Triangular arbitrage trading enforces the no-arbitrage principle in FX markets by immediately exploiting and eliminating any inconsistency in quoted cross rates.

label: instance of
description: Triangular arbitrage is a specific instance of riskless arbitrage applied to FX markets — inconsistent cross rates allow a three-legged trade that returns a risk-free profit with no net currency exposure.

label: input to
description: The underlying price is the primary input to the Black-Scholes equation — the spot price S enters both the closed-form option price and the PDE that governs derivative valuation.

label: drives sensitivity
description: Changes in the underlying price are what the Greeks measure — delta quantifies the first-order sensitivity of option value to the underlying, and gamma captures the second-order effect.

label: determines
description: Moneyness is defined by the relationship between the underlying price and the strike price — whether an option is in, at, or out of the money depends entirely on the current level of S relative to K.

label: priced by
description: The Black-Scholes equation provides the closed-form formula for the value of a European call option, expressing the price as a function of the underlying, strike, volatility, rate, and time to expiry.

label: differentiated to give
description: The option Greeks are partial derivatives of the call value with respect to its inputs — Delta, Gamma, Theta, Vega, and Rho each measure how the call price responds to a small change in one parameter.

label: linked via
description: Put-call parity ties the call value to the put value through the no-arbitrage relation C - P = S - Ke^{-rT}, so knowing one option value immediately determines the other.

label: counterpart of
description: Call and put values are mirror-image payoffs on the same underlying — calls profit when the asset rises above the strike while puts profit when it falls below, and they are linked exactly by put-call parity.

label: priced by
description: The Black-Scholes equation provides the closed-form formula for the value of a European put option, using the same d1 and d2 terms as the call but with negated normal CDF arguments.

label: differentiated to give
description: The put value's partial derivatives yield the put-specific Greeks — put Delta is negative (N(d1) - 1), while Gamma, Vega, and Rho take corresponding signs reflecting the put's downside exposure.

label: linked via
description: Put-call parity allows the put value to be derived directly from the call value and vice versa, ensuring no-arbitrage consistency between the two option types.

label: paired with
description: Vanna and Volga together form the basis of the Vanna-Volga pricing method, which adjusts Black-Scholes prices for the volatility smile using second-order volatility and cross sensitivities.

label: analogous to
description: Vanna and Zomma both capture cross-sensitivities with volatility: Vanna measures how Delta changes with volatility, while Zomma measures how Gamma changes with volatility.

label: cross-derivative of
description: Vanna measures how Vega changes with the underlying price — it is the same cross partial derivative viewed from the volatility sensitivity side.

label: differentiated to give
description: Veta is the time derivative of Vega — it measures how an option s volatility sensitivity decays as expiry approaches, governing the term structure of Vega across maturities.

label: differentiated to give
description: Volga is the derivative of Vega with respect to implied volatility — it measures how Vega itself changes as volatility moves, capturing the curvature of the option price in volatility space.

label: mitigated by clearing at
description: Central counterparty clearing mitigates XVA by mutualising counterparty risk — trades cleared through a CCP have near-zero CVA because the CCP guarantees performance through its default waterfall.

label: reduced by
description: XVA charges are reduced by collateral agreements (CSAs) — posting variation margin eliminates most of the exposure that drives CVA and FVA, which is why collateralised trades have much lower XVA than uncollateralised ones.

label: prices
description: XVA prices counterparty risk into derivatives valuations — CVA is the market-consistent cost of the counterparty s default risk, transforming a theoretical concern into a concrete P&L charge.

label: computed via
description: XVA computation requires Monte Carlo simulation of the entire trade portfolio across thousands of future scenarios to estimate the expected exposure profiles that drive CVA, DVA, and FVA.

label: objective of
description: Absolute return is the defining objective of hedge funds — unlike traditional funds benchmarked against an index, hedge funds aim to deliver positive returns regardless of market direction.

label: contrasts with
description: Absolute and relative return represent two fundamentally different ways to measure investment success — one asks 'did you make money?' while the other asks 'did you beat the benchmark?'.

label: measured by
description: The Sharpe ratio evaluates absolute return strategies by dividing excess return over the risk-free rate by portfolio volatility — it is the standard metric for comparing absolute return managers.

label: manages
description: ALM is the operational function responsible for managing IRRBB — it monitors EVE and NII sensitivities, executes hedges, and ensures compliance with regulatory limits.

label: manages
description: The duration gap is the primary structural metric that ALM teams monitor and adjust — narrowing or widening the gap via swaps or balance sheet changes is the core operational activity of ALM.

label: optimises
description: ALM optimises NII by managing the repricing profile of the banking book — the ALM desk decides how much rate risk to take in pursuit of higher net interest margins while staying within risk limits.

label: defines
