description: The binomial coefficient C(n,k) = n\! / (k\!(n−k)\!) uses factorial, which is defined via product notation Π — counting the number of paths with exactly k successes is central to the binomial distribution.

label: notation for
description: For independent random variables the MGF factorises as M_{X+Y}(t) = M_X(t) · M_Y(t) — product notation compactly expresses the factorisation M_{Σ Xᵢ}(t) = Π M_{Xᵢ}(t) that is key to proving the CLT.

label: contains
description: The real numbers R contain all integers as a special case — the inclusion Z ⊂ R is fundamental to understanding why continuous financial models (which live in R) can be discretised into integer-indexed time steps or contract counts.

label: domain of
description: A probability density function is a function f: R → [0,∞) — the real number line is the natural domain of the PDF for continuous random variables such as asset returns, log-prices, and interest rates.

label: foundation of
description: Logical quantifiers operate over sets — ‘for all x ∈ S’ and ‘there exists x ∈ S’ require set membership notation, making set notation the prerequisite for writing and reading quantified mathematical statements.

label: notation for
description: Bayes’ theorem P(A|B) = P(B|A)P(A)/P(B) is built on conditional probability P(A ∩ B)/P(B) — set intersection, complement, and partition notation are essential for stating and applying the theorem correctly.

label: notation for
description: Probability is defined on sets of outcomes within the sample space Ω — the PDF assigns density to events (measurable sets), and expressions like P(X ∈ A) = ∫_A f(x) dx require set notation to specify the domain of integration.

label: extends
description: The stochastic integral uses the same integral sign as the Riemann integral but requires an entirely different construction due to the infinite variation of Brownian paths — ordinary integration theory cannot handle dW.

label: used in
description: Ito's lemma generates stochastic differential equations whose solutions are expressed as stochastic integrals — the notation int_0^t sigma(S_s) dW_s appears directly in every SDE solution and delta-hedging P&L formula.

label: notation for
description: The stochastic integrals card covers the probabilistic construction and properties of the Ito integral; this notation card explains how to read the symbols int H dW, the adaptedness requirement, and the Ito versus Stratonovich conventions.

label: discrete analogue of
description: Summation is the discrete counterpart of integration — Riemann integration is defined as the limit of finite sums Σ f(xᵢ) Δx as Δx → 0, making summation notation the conceptual bridge to continuous calculus.

label: notation for
description: Portfolio return E[Rₚ] = Σ wᵢ μᵢ and portfolio variance σₚ² = ΣΣ wᵢ wⱼ σᵢⱼ use single and double summation notation — Markowitz optimisation is expressed entirely in terms of these sums.

label: notation for
description: The discrete expected value E[X] = Σ xᵢ P(X = xᵢ) is a direct application of summation notation — sigma notation compactly expresses the probability-weighted sum over all outcomes.

label: notation for
description: Variance Var(X) = Σ (xᵢ − μ)² P(X = xᵢ) uses summation notation to express the expected squared deviation — understanding index variables and bounds is essential for computing sample and population variance.

label: measured against
description: Active share quantifies the percentage of holdings that differ from the benchmark — without a defined benchmark index, the concept has no meaning.

label: predicts
description: Research shows that funds with high active share tend to generate higher relative returns after fees — closet indexers with low active share consistently underperform their benchmarks.

label: complements
description: Active share and tracking error together provide a two-dimensional view of active management — active share measures how different the holdings are, while tracking error measures how different the returns are.

label: evaluated by
description: Hedge fund investors decompose returns into alpha (manager skill) and beta (market exposure) to determine whether fees are justified by genuine stock-picking ability.

label: derived from
description: Alpha and beta originate directly from the CAPM equation — beta is the slope of the security market line and alpha is any return that deviates from it.

label: generalised by
description: The Fama–French model extends the single-factor alpha-beta decomposition to three factors, redefining alpha as the return unexplained by market, size, and value exposures.

label: estimated via
description: Beta is estimated by regressing asset returns against market returns — the slope coefficient is the beta and the intercept is the realised alpha over the estimation window.

label: motivates
description: Alpha decay motivates optimal execution — the faster the signal decays, the more urgently the order must be executed, shifting the optimal trajectory from patient VWAP towards aggressive immediate execution.

label: limits profitability of
description: Alpha decay limits the profitability of momentum strategies as more participants trade the same signals — crowding accelerates the price adjustment and shortens the signal half-life.

label: traded off against
description: Alpha decay must be traded off against transaction costs — executing faster captures more alpha but incurs higher market impact, and the optimal speed balances the marginal alpha saved against the marginal cost incurred.

label: derived from
description: APT is built on the no-arbitrage principle — if an asset's expected return does not equal the sum of its factor risk premia, a portfolio of many assets can be constructed to earn riskless profit, which the market eliminates.

label: generalises
description: APT generalises CAPM by allowing multiple systematic risk factors rather than a single market factor — CAPM is the special case of APT where the only priced factor is the market portfolio.

label: alternative to
description: While MPT and CAPM derive asset pricing from equilibrium assumptions about investor behaviour, APT reaches similar conclusions using only the weaker no-arbitrage condition, making it a more general alternative framework.

label: uses
description: The equilibrium returns Π = δΣw are derived directly from the covariance matrix — Black-Litterman requires a well-estimated Σ as its starting point for computing implied returns.

label: stabilises
description: Black-Litterman produces stable expected returns that, when fed into Markowitz optimisation, generate sensible portfolios on the efficient frontier — unlike raw return estimates which cause the frontier to shift erratically.

label: extends
description: Black-Litterman is a Bayesian extension of Markowitz that solves the practical problem of estimating expected returns — it anchors to market equilibrium and tilts towards investor views, making mean-variance optimisation usable in practice.

label: evaluates
description: The Calmar ratio is widely used to evaluate hedge fund and CTA performance, particularly for trend-following strategies where drawdown discipline is more important than day-to-day volatility.

label: alternative to
description: The Calmar ratio uses maximum drawdown as the risk denominator instead of volatility — it penalises the worst cumulative loss rather than average dispersion, making it more relevant for strategies where drawdown is the binding constraint.

label: denominator is
description: Maximum drawdown appears in the denominator of the Calmar ratio — it anchors the risk-adjusted performance measure to the worst peak-to-trough loss the strategy actually experienced.

label: models
description: CAPM models the expected return of an asset as a linear function of its systematic risk — the return on investment is the quantity that CAPM predicts.

label: anchored by
description: The CAPM expected return formula E[r] = r_f + beta(E[r_m] - r_f) is anchored by the risk-free rate, which sets the baseline return for bearing zero systematic risk.

label: assumes complete
description: The CAPM assumes investors are fully diversified, holding the market portfolio, so only systematic risk (which cannot be diversified away) is compensated with expected return.

label: depends on
description: Beta is the ratio of the covariance between asset and market returns to market variance — computing beta requires estimating the correlation between the asset and the market portfolio.

label: computed from
description: Beta equals Cov(R_i, R_m) / Var(R_m) — the market variance in the denominator normalises the covariance to produce a unitless sensitivity measure.

label: foundation of
description: CAPM is derived by assuming all investors hold portfolios on the CML — the tangency portfolio becomes the market portfolio, and the CML slope becomes the market price of risk.

label: dominates
description: The CML is a straight line from the risk-free rate through the tangency portfolio that lies above the curved efficient frontier at every risk level — adding a risk-free asset strictly improves the attainable risk-return trade-off.

label: slope equals
description: The slope of the CML is the Sharpe ratio of the tangency portfolio — the maximum attainable Sharpe ratio among all portfolios of risky assets.

label: framework for
description: The Carhart four-factor model provides the standard academic framework for factor investing, decomposing returns into systematic factor exposures and residual alpha.

label: redefines
description: The Carhart model redefines alpha by adding momentum as an explanatory factor — returns that appear as alpha under CAPM or three-factor models may be reclassified as compensated momentum exposure.

label: extends
description: The Carhart model extends the Fama-French three-factor model by adding the momentum factor (WML), capturing return persistence that the market, size, and value factors leave unexplained.

label: formalises
description: The Carhart model formalises the momentum factor as a systematic risk premium within an asset pricing framework, establishing WML as the fourth standard factor alongside market, size, and value.

label: cross-sectional form is
description: Cross-sectional momentum is naturally implemented as a dollar-neutral long-short equity strategy, going long relative winners and short relative losers with zero net market exposure.

label: contrasts with
description: Both cross-sectional and time-series momentum contrast with mean reversion strategies — momentum bets on continuation while mean reversion bets on reversal, and the choice between them depends on the horizon and asset class.

label: decomposes
description: Cross-sectional vs time-series momentum decomposes the broad momentum effect into two distinct implementations — relative ranking within a universe versus absolute trend signals on individual assets.

label: enables
description: Diversification enables the efficient frontier — by combining imperfectly correlated assets, investors can achieve risk-return combinations that are superior to any individual asset.

label: driven by
description: Diversification benefit is driven by imperfect correlation between assets — the lower the correlation, the greater the reduction in portfolio variance from combining them.

label: reduces
description: Diversification reduces portfolio variance below the weighted average of individual asset variances whenever correlations are less than 1, which is the mathematical core of the diversification effect.

label: output of
description: The efficient frontier is the geometric output of Markowitz optimisation — it plots the set of portfolios that offer maximum return for each level of risk, summarising the entire mean-variance trade-off.

label: maximised at tangency
description: The tangency portfolio, where a line from the risk-free rate is tangent to the efficient frontier, is the portfolio with the maximum Sharpe ratio — the optimal mix of risky assets for any investor.

label: minimises
description: Each point on the efficient frontier is a portfolio that minimises variance for its target return — the portfolio variance formula is the objective function solved at every point along the curve.

label: extends
description: The Fama–French model adds size (SMB) and value (HML) factors to the single market factor of CAPM, explaining cross-sectional return patterns that CAPM alone attributes incorrectly to alpha.

label: estimated via
description: Factor loadings in the Fama–French model are estimated by regressing excess returns on the three factor time series — the regression minimises the variance of the residual to find the best-fitting sensitivities.

label: justifies breadth of
description: The Fundamental Law provides the theoretical justification for factor investing s approach of making many small systematic bets across hundreds of securities rather than concentrating in a few positions.

label: decomposes
description: The Fundamental Law decomposes the information ratio into forecast accuracy (IC) and breadth (BR), revealing the two independent levers a portfolio manager can use to improve risk-adjusted active returns.

label: constrains
description: The Fundamental Law implies that for a given IC and breadth, the optimal tracking error budget determines how aggressively active bets should be sized — exceeding it adds risk without proportional return.

label: normalises
description: The information ratio divides average relative return by tracking error — it measures how much active return a manager earns per unit of active risk taken against the benchmark.

label: analogous to
description: The information ratio is to relative return what the Sharpe ratio is to absolute return — both divide excess return by its volatility, but the IR uses the benchmark as the baseline instead of the risk-free rate.

label: denominator is
description: Tracking error appears in the denominator of the information ratio — a lower tracking error for the same active return produces a higher IR, rewarding consistency of outperformance.

label: formalises
description: Jensen's alpha is the statistical formalisation of the alpha concept — it provides a regression-based estimate with standard errors and significance tests, turning alpha from an informal notion into a testable hypothesis.

label: tests
description: Jensen's alpha tests the CAPM prediction that all assets should lie on the Security Market Line — a statistically significant alpha implies the CAPM misprices the asset or the manager has genuine skill.

label: estimated alongside
description: Jensen's alpha and beta are jointly estimated by regressing portfolio excess returns on market excess returns — alpha is the intercept and beta is the slope of the same regression.

label: foundation of
description: Pairs trading is a direct application of mean reversion — it identifies two cointegrated securities and trades the spread when it deviates from its historical mean, expecting convergence.

label: contrarian complement to
description: Mean reversion is the contrarian complement to momentum — it profits from overreaction and price correction, while momentum profits from underreaction and trend continuation. The two strategies tend to perform well in opposite market regimes.

label: detected via
description: Negative autocorrelation in returns at short horizons is the statistical signature that mean reversion strategies exploit — it indicates that positive returns tend to be followed by negative ones and vice versa.

label: depends only on
description: The MVP weights are Σ⁻¹·¹ / (¹ᵀΣ⁻¹·¹) — they require only the covariance matrix, not expected returns, making the MVP the most robust optimal portfolio against return estimation error.

label: leftmost point of
description: The MVP is the leftmost point on the efficient frontier — the portfolio with the absolute lowest variance, dividing the frontier into an upper (efficient) and lower (inefficient) branch.

label: minimises globally
description: The MVP is the unconstrained global minimum of the portfolio variance function wᵀΣw subject only to full investment — no return target is imposed.

label: computed via
description: Portfolio variance is the quadratic form w'Σw and expected return is the dot product w'μ — both are matrix multiplications, making linear algebra the computational engine of portfolio optimisation.

label: foundation of
description: CAPM is derived from Modern Portfolio Theory by asking what expected returns must hold in equilibrium when all investors hold mean-variance efficient portfolios — it is the single-factor equilibrium result of MPT.

label: feeds into
description: The portfolio variance from MPT is the key input to parametric VaR — once mean-variance weights are chosen, the portfolio standard deviation is plugged directly into the VaR formula to quantify downside risk.

label: depends on
description: Portfolio variance is determined by the pairwise correlations between assets — the off-diagonal terms of the covariance matrix drive the diversification benefit that is the central result of MPT.

label: minimises
description: MPT defines risk as portfolio variance and seeks the weight vector that minimises it for a given target return — variance is the objective function at the heart of mean-variance optimisation.

label: benefits
description: Momentum crashes tend to benefit mean reversion strategies, as the sharp reversal of past losers is exactly the kind of price correction that contrarian approaches profit from.

label: tail risk of
description: Momentum crashes are the primary tail risk of momentum strategies — sudden reversals where past losers sharply outperform past winners can erase years of accumulated profits in weeks.

label: core factor of
description: Momentum is one of the most widely implemented factor strategies — it forms a core building block in multi-factor portfolios alongside value, size, and quality.

label: implemented as
description: Cross-sectional momentum is typically implemented as a long-short equity strategy — going long recent winners and short recent losers to isolate the momentum premium from broad market exposure.

label: extends
description: Adding the momentum factor (WML) to the Fama–French three-factor model produces the Carhart four-factor model, which captures the return persistence that the original three factors leave unexplained.

label: generalises
description: The Omega ratio captures the entire return distribution including skewness and kurtosis, while the Sharpe ratio uses only mean and variance — for normally distributed returns they rank strategies identically, but they diverge for non-normal distributions.

label: computed from
description: The Omega ratio is defined as the ratio of integrals of the CDF above and below the threshold — it directly uses the cumulative distribution function to weight gains against losses.

label: sensitive to
description: The Omega ratio captures fat-tail effects that the Sharpe ratio ignores — a strategy with positive skew and thin left tails will have a much higher Omega ratio than one with negative skew and fat left tails, even if their Sharpe ratios are similar.

label: evaluated by
description: A portfolio manager s performance is evaluated using the Carhart four-factor model — only the alpha that survives adjustment for market, size, value, and momentum exposures counts as genuine skill.

label: optimises along
description: A portfolio manager aims to construct portfolios on or near the efficient frontier, maximising expected return for a given level of risk through optimal asset allocation.

label: governed by
description: A portfolio manager s potential performance is governed by the Fundamental Law — it sets the theoretical ceiling on achievable information ratio given the manager s forecast skill and number of independent bets.

label: skill quantified by
description: The information ratio quantifies a portfolio manager s skill by measuring active return per unit of tracking error relative to the benchmark — it is the standard metric for active management performance.

label: implements
description: Rebalancing is one of the core implementation decisions a portfolio manager makes — the choice of method (calendar, threshold, or optimised) directly affects portfolio risk, turnover, and net returns.

label: measured by
description: The Sharpe ratio is one of the primary metrics used to assess a portfolio manager s risk-adjusted performance, measuring excess return per unit of total volatility.

label: maintains position on
description: Rebalancing maintains the portfolio s position on or near the efficient frontier by correcting drift that pushes the actual allocation away from the optimally diversified target weights.

label: implicitly contrarian like
description: Rebalancing is implicitly a contrarian strategy — it systematically sells assets that have appreciated (overweight) and buys those that have declined (underweight), capturing a rebalancing premium in mean-reverting markets.

label: cost evaluated by
description: The cost of rebalancing trades is evaluated using transaction cost analysis — the optimal rebalancing policy balances drift-reduction benefits against the market impact and spread costs of trading.

label: stabilises
description: The resampled frontier averages portfolio weights across many bootstrapped frontiers, producing more diversified and stable allocations than the classical frontier computed from a single set of noisy inputs.

label: simulates from
description: Each resample draws returns from a multivariate normal calibrated to the original estimates — the normality assumption enables fast bootstrapping of alternative efficient frontiers.

label: alternative to
description: Both resampling and shrinkage address estimation error in Markowitz optimisation, but through different mechanisms — resampling averages over input uncertainty while shrinkage biases the inputs towards a stable target.

label: extends
description: Risk budgeting extends mean-variance optimisation by expressing the objective in risk-contribution units rather than capital weights — it produces more robust allocations because it avoids sensitivity to expected return estimates.

label: generalises
description: Risk parity is the special case of risk budgeting where every asset receives an equal share of the risk budget — risk budgeting allows deliberate tilts towards assets with higher expected risk-adjusted returns.

label: denominated in
description: Risk contributions are expressed in units of portfolio volatility — each asset's marginal contribution to standard deviation determines its share of the total risk budget.

label: requires
description: Risk parity portfolios are heavily weighted towards low-volatility assets like bonds, so they use leverage to raise the expected return to competitive levels — without leverage, the strategy would deliver bond-like returns.

label: extends
description: Risk parity extends mean-variance optimisation by defining the objective as equal risk contribution from each asset, rather than minimising variance for a given return target.

label: depends on
description: Risk parity allocations are computed from the covariance matrix — when correlations between assets spike (as in a crisis), the equal-risk-contribution assumption breaks down and the portfolio becomes less diversified than intended.

label: visualises
description: The Security Market Line is the graphical representation of the CAPM equation — it plots expected return against beta, making the linear risk-return trade-off visually explicit.

label: uses
description: The numerator of the Sharpe Ratio is excess return — the portfolio return minus the risk-free rate — so computing the Sharpe Ratio begins with calculating returns.

label: benchmarked against
description: The Sharpe ratio measures excess return per unit of risk, where excess return is defined as portfolio return minus the risk-free rate — the choice of r_f directly affects the ratio.

label: related to
description: Both the Sharpe Ratio and CAPM use the risk-free rate as a baseline — CAPM prices expected return via systematic risk, while the Sharpe Ratio evaluates realised performance per unit of total risk.

label: optimised by
description: The tangency portfolio on the efficient frontier is the portfolio that maximises the Sharpe Ratio — mean-variance optimisation and Sharpe maximisation are equivalent when a risk-free asset is available.

label: normalised by
description: The denominator of the Sharpe Ratio is the standard deviation of portfolio returns — it normalises excess return by volatility to produce a risk-adjusted performance measure.

label: evaluates
description: The Sortino ratio evaluates absolute return strategies by penalising only downside volatility — it is more appropriate than the Sharpe ratio for strategies with asymmetric return distributions.

label: refines
description: The Sortino ratio modifies the Sharpe ratio by replacing total standard deviation with downside deviation — it rewards strategies that achieve high returns with large upside moves but small downside moves.

label: modifies
description: The Sortino ratio replaces standard deviation with semi-deviation (downside only), recognising that investors treat upside and downside volatility asymmetrically.

label: smooths
description: The Sterling ratio uses the average of the largest drawdowns instead of the single worst drawdown — this smooths the denominator and produces a less volatile risk-adjusted metric than the Calmar ratio.

label: alternative to
description: The Sterling ratio uses average drawdown as the risk denominator instead of standard deviation — it is more intuitive for investors who think about risk in terms of actual losses rather than statistical dispersion.

label: averages over
description: While the Calmar ratio uses the single maximum drawdown, the Sterling ratio averages the largest drawdowns — this reduces the influence of a single extreme event on the risk assessment.

label: measured against
description: Tracking error quantifies how closely a portfolio follows its benchmark index — without a defined benchmark, the concept of tracking error has no meaning.

label: volatility of
description: Tracking error is the standard deviation of relative returns over time — it measures the consistency of the active return series rather than its average level.

label: instance of
description: Tracking error is simply the standard deviation applied to the time series of active returns (portfolio minus benchmark) — it uses the same formula but on a derived return series.

label: decomposes
description: Transaction cost analysis decomposes execution costs into components, of which the bid-ask spread (half-spread crossing cost) is the most predictable and often the largest for small orders.

label: quantified by
description: Market impact models (such as the square-root model) are the quantitative core of transaction cost analysis, estimating how much a trade s own execution moves the price against the trader.

label: erodes
description: Transaction costs directly erode alpha — a signal that generates 50 bps of gross alpha but costs 40 bps to execute delivers only 10 bps net, making TCA essential for determining whether active management adds value.

label: feedback loop for
description: Transaction cost analysis provides the feedback loop that portfolio managers use to evaluate execution quality, refine trading algorithms, and determine whether signals remain profitable net of costs.

label: denominator is
description: Beta appears in the denominator of the Treynor ratio — it measures excess return per unit of systematic risk, making beta estimation accuracy critical to the metric's reliability.

label: derived from
description: The Treynor ratio is the CAPM-implied reward per unit of beta — a portfolio on the Security Market Line has a Treynor ratio equal to the market risk premium.

label: complements
description: The Treynor ratio uses beta (systematic risk) as the denominator while the Sharpe ratio uses total volatility — they agree for fully diversified portfolios but diverge when idiosyncratic risk is present.

label: foundation of
description: CAPM is derived by assuming all investors satisfy two-fund separation — if everyone holds the tangency portfolio, it must equal the market portfolio, yielding the CAPM pricing equation.

label: implies
description: Two-fund separation is the algebraic result that all CML portfolios are combinations of the risk-free asset and the tangency portfolio — the CML is the geometric expression of the theorem.

label: key result of
description: Two-fund separation is the central structural result of Markowitz theory with a risk-free asset — it reduces the infinite-dimensional portfolio choice to a single parameter (the allocation to risky assets).

label: foundation of
description: Black-Litterman is a Bayesian approach that combines market equilibrium returns (the prior) with investor views (the likelihood) to produce a posterior distribution of expected returns — this overcomes the instability of traditional mean-variance optimisation.

label: updates
description: Bayes' Theorem updates your probability distribution as new information arrives; the expected value computed from that updated distribution is your revised estimate of fair value.

label: prices via
description: The binomial tree model prices American options by working backwards through a recombining tree — at each node the number of up-moves follows a binomial distribution, and early exercise is checked at every step.

label: applied in
description: The terminal stock price distribution in an N-step recombining binomial tree follows a binomial distribution with parameters N and p-prime, connecting discrete probability theory directly to option pricing.

label: defines mean of
description: The expected value of a binomial random variable is np — this simple formula gives the average number of up-moves in a binomial tree, which determines the expected terminal stock price.

label: converges to
description: By the Central Limit Theorem, the binomial distribution approaches the normal distribution as n grows — this convergence is why the binomial tree model reproduces Black-Scholes prices in the continuous limit.

label: limiting case of
description: When the number of trials n is large and the probability p is small, the binomial distribution converges to the Poisson — this limit is used in credit risk to model rare default events in large portfolios.

label: illustrates
description: The binomial distribution is the textbook illustration of the CLT — the sum of n independent Bernoulli trials becomes approximately normal as n grows, which is exactly the convergence that turns a binomial tree into Black-Scholes.

label: counts steps of
description: Each step of a discrete random walk is an independent up-or-down trial — the total number of up-moves after n steps follows a binomial distribution B(n, p).

label: alternative to
description: Box-Muller avoids the costly evaluation of Phi_inverse required by inverse transform sampling, using only logarithms and trigonometric functions to produce normal draws.

label: generates
description: The Box-Muller transform produces exact Z ~ N(0,1) samples from uniform random inputs, providing the standard normal draws needed for simulation.

label: powers
description: Monte Carlo pricing engines use Box-Muller (or its polar variant) to generate the millions of standard normal draws required for simulating asset price paths.

label: notation explained by
description: N(x) and N'(x) in Black-Scholes are the CDF and PDF of the standard normal — this comparison card explains why one gives probabilities directly while the other requires integration.

label: compares
description: The CDF card defines F(x) = P(X ≤ x) in detail — this comparison card explains how it contrasts with the PDF and when each is the right tool in quantitative finance.

label: foundation of
description: Inverse transform sampling relies on the CDF and its inverse to convert uniform random numbers into any target distribution — understanding the CDF-PDF relationship is prerequisite to understanding why this technique works.

label: compares
description: The PDF card defines f(x) and its properties — this comparison card explains the integration-differentiation duality between PDF and CDF and when each appears in finance.

label: built from
description: The chi-squared distribution is the sum of squared standard normals — it arises naturally when testing whether the variance of normally distributed returns has changed.

label: component of
description: The t-distribution is constructed as a standard normal divided by the square root of a chi-squared divided by its degrees of freedom — the two distributions are algebraically inseparable in small-sample inference.

label: distribution of
description: The Jarque-Bera test statistic follows a chi-squared distribution with 2 degrees of freedom under the null hypothesis of normality — the chi-squared CDF provides the p-value used to accept or reject.

label: tests
description: The chi-squared test statistic (n−1)s²/σ₀² follows a chi-squared distribution under the null hypothesis — this is the standard method for testing whether realised volatility matches a hypothesised value.

label: uses
description: Copulas are defined in terms of uniform marginals obtained by applying the CDF to each variable — Sklar's theorem decomposes any joint CDF into marginal CDFs and a copula.

label: generalises dependence of
description: The Gaussian copula reproduces multivariate normal dependence but copulas in general allow non-elliptical, asymmetric dependencies — capturing tail behaviour that the multivariate normal misses.

label: improves tail modelling of
description: Copulas with non-zero tail dependence produce more realistic joint loss distributions for portfolio VaR, correcting the systematic underestimation of joint extreme events by Gaussian models.

label: corrects for
description: The expansion adds skewness and kurtosis correction terms to the normal quantile, capturing the key features of fat-tailed return distributions without fitting a full parametric model.

label: extends
description: The Cornish-Fisher expansion corrects the standard normal quantile Phi_inverse(alpha) for skewness and excess kurtosis, generalising the probit function to non-normal distributions.

label: adjusts
description: Modified VaR uses the Cornish-Fisher expansion to correct the Gaussian quantile for non-normality, producing more accurate risk estimates for skewed and fat-tailed portfolios.

label: applied in
description: The Black-Scholes formula uses Φ(d₁) and Φ(d₂) — the cumulative normal distribution function — to compute the option price as a weighted combination of probabilities.

label: used in
description: Delta for a European call equals N(d1), where N is the cumulative standard normal distribution function — computing Delta therefore requires evaluating the CDF at d1.

label: integrates
description: The CDF is the integral of the PDF — F(x) accumulates the density from −∞ to x, and conversely the PDF is the derivative of the CDF.

label: specialised as
description: The standardised normal distribution function Phi(z) is the CDF specialised to the standard normal Z ~ N(0,1) — the most important single instance of a cumulative distribution function in quantitative finance.

label: inverted via
description: The inverse CDF (quantile function) transforms a uniform U(0,1) draw into a sample from any target distribution — this inverse transform method is the bridge between uniform random numbers and Monte Carlo simulation.

label: defines quantile of
description: Value at Risk is defined as a quantile of the loss distribution — it is the inverse CDF evaluated at the confidence level, making the CDF the mathematical object that directly determines VaR.

label: computed within
description: Production Black-Scholes implementations evaluate N(d1) and N(d2) through optimised rational approximations to the error function, making erf the numerical bottleneck in option pricing.

label: implements
description: Every numerical evaluation of Phi(z) reduces to computing the error function via Phi(z) = (1 + erf(z/sqrt(2)))/2, making erf the computational engine behind normal distribution calculations.

label: foundation of
description: Expected value is the mathematical operation at the heart of theoretical pricing — the theoretical value of a derivative is a discounted, measure-changed expectation of its payoff.

label: discounted by
description: A fair price equals the expected future payoff discounted back to today — combining expected value with time-value-of-money discounting is the core of risk-neutral option pricing.

label: mirrors
description: The survival probability e^{−λt} has the same exponential form as the continuous discount factor e^{−rt} — default intensity in credit risk plays an analogous role to the risk-free rate in time-value-of-money discounting.

label: inter-arrival times of
description: If events arrive as a Poisson process with rate λ, the waiting time between consecutive events follows an exponential distribution with the same rate — the two distributions are inseparable.

label: contrasts with
description: Fat-tailed distributions are defined by comparison with the normal — the normal's exponentially decaying tails assign negligibly small probabilities to extreme events, while fat-tailed distributions assign far greater probability, making the normal the baseline that fat tails violate.

label: motivates
description: The presence of fat tails in real return data is the single most important empirical fact driving model limitations — any model that assumes normality will systematically underestimate the frequency and severity of extreme losses.

label: undermines
description: Parametric VaR computed under a normal assumption understates tail losses because fat-tailed returns produce extreme quantiles that are far larger than the Gaussian z-score implies. Fat tails push more probability mass into extreme losses, so the true 99th-percentile loss exceeds the Gaussian estimate — switching from a normal to a fat-tailed model systematically increases VaR at high confidence levels.

label: motivates
description: The fat tails observed in equity return distributions cannot be generated by pure Brownian diffusion — jump-diffusion models were introduced specifically to produce the excess kurtosis and negative skewness that fat-tailed data demands.

label: derived from
description: The hazard rate h(t) = f(t)/(1 - F(t)) is computed from the CDF and PDF, repackaging distributional information into a conditional failure rate.

label: constant case gives
description: A constant hazard rate lambda produces the exponential distribution with survival function e^(-lambda*t), the simplest and most widely used default intensity model.

label: models
description: Reduced-form credit models use the hazard rate as the default intensity, calibrated from CDS spreads to compute counterparty default probabilities and credit valuation adjustments.

label: inverts
description: Inverse transform sampling works by inverting the CDF: if U ~ Uniform(0,1), then F^{-1}(U) has distribution F. The CDF is the key ingredient that makes this method exact.

label: applied to
description: To generate standard normal draws, inverse transform sampling applies the inverse normal CDF (probit function) to uniform random numbers — this is the foundation of all SDE simulation.

label: uses
description: Inverse transform sampling starts from uniform random numbers on [0,1] and transforms them into draws from any target distribution via the inverse CDF.

label: input to
description: Every Euler-Maruyama step requires a draw from N(0,1), which is generated via inverse transform sampling — applying the inverse normal CDF to a uniform random number.

label: explains
description: Jensen's inequality explains why the expected value of an option (a convex payoff) is greater than the payoff of the expected value, a fundamental driver of option premiums.

label: underlies
description: Jensen s inequality explains why diversification works: the portfolio variance (a convex function of weights) is reduced by averaging, and the concavity of utility functions means investors prefer less uncertainty.

label: constrains
description: Jensen s inequality constrains the relationship between E[f(X)] and f(E[X]) based on the convexity of f — for convex f the expected value of the function exceeds the function of the expected value.

label: foundation of
description: Jensen s inequality for the concave log function gives E[ln X] <= ln E[X], which is the mathematical basis for volatility drag — randomness always reduces the compound growth rate.

label: explains pricing of
description: Jensen's inequality applied to the concave square-root function explains why the fair volatility swap strike is below the square root of the variance swap strike — E[sigma] < sqrt(E[sigma^2]).

label: extends to multivariate
description: The CDF-vs-PDF relationship extends to multiple dimensions — the joint CDF is the integral of the joint PDF, and marginal PDFs are obtained by integrating out the other variables.

label: decomposed by
description: Sklar's theorem decomposes any joint distribution into its marginals and a copula that captures the dependence structure — understanding the joint PDF is prerequisite to understanding why copulas are needed.

label: parameterised by
description: For the bivariate normal, the correlation coefficient ρ fully parameterises the joint PDF given the marginals — but for non-normal joints, ρ alone does not capture the full dependence structure.

label: assumed by
description: Black-Scholes assumes stock prices are lognormally distributed — the N(d₁) and N(d₂) terms in the formula are computed from the normal distribution of log-prices.

label: reciprocal of
description: Mill s ratio is the reciprocal of the normal hazard rate — a high Mill s ratio at z means the tail decays slowly relative to the density at that point.

label: ratio involving
description: Mill s ratio is defined as phi(z)/(1 - Phi(z)), the ratio of the normal PDF to the survival function, connecting density and tail probability.

label: approximates tail of
description: Mill s ratio provides the leading-order tail approximation 1 - Phi(z) ~ phi(z)/z for large z, enabling quick estimates of extreme normal tail probabilities.

label: generalises
description: The MGF encodes all moments of a distribution — the first derivative at zero gives the expected value, and higher derivatives give higher moments, making it a compact summary of the entire moment sequence.

label: characterises
description: The normal distribution's MGF exp(μt + ½σ²t²) uniquely identifies it — this is the tool used to prove that the sum of independent normals is again normal, a result that underpins portfolio variance calculations.

label: extracts
description: The variance is recovered from the MGF via Var(X) = M''(0) − [M'(0)]² — the MGF packages the first and second moments needed to compute volatility into a single generating function.

label: parameterised by
description: The multivariate normal is fully specified by its mean vector and covariance matrix — the covariance matrix encodes all pairwise dependencies, making it the sufficient statistic for joint normality.

label: generalises
description: The univariate normal is a special case of the multivariate normal with n=1 — the multivariate version extends the bell-curve concept to vectors of random variables with correlated components.

label: assumed by
description: Parametric VaR assumes portfolio returns are normally distributed — the multivariate normal justifies computing VaR from just the portfolio mean and variance without simulating the full return distribution.

label: N(d1), N(d2) from
description: The Black-Scholes formula contains N(d₁) and N(d₂), which are cumulative normal probabilities representing the option's Delta and the risk-neutral probability of finishing in the money. Black–Scholes assumes log-returns are normally distributed via the GBM model — this distributional assumption is distinct from the formula’s mechanical use of N(d₁) and N(d₂), and is the reason the model underprices tail risk.

label: exponentiates to
description: If log-returns are normally distributed then the price itself is lognormally distributed — the lognormal is simply the exponential of a normal random variable.

label: evaluated via
description: Any normal probability P(X <= x) is computed by standardising to a z-score and evaluating Phi(z), reducing all normal distribution calculations to a single tabulated function.

label: standardised via
description: Any normal variable X ~ N(mu, sigma^2) is converted to the standardised normal variable Z = (X - mu)/sigma, reducing all normal distribution problems to a single universal scale.

label: used in
description: Parametric VaR assumes returns are normally distributed, so the 1% or 5% loss quantile is simply the mean minus a fixed multiple of the standard deviation (1.645σ for 95%, 2.326σ for 99%). Parametric VaR assumes portfolio returns are normally distributed, placing all tail-risk information into the variance — this is a modelling assumption distinct from the normal distribution being used as a computational tool in VaR calculations.

label: null hypothesis of
description: The Jarque-Bera test's null hypothesis is that the data are normally distributed — it checks if skewness and excess kurtosis are both zero, as they are for the Gaussian.

label: fits
description: Standardised daily returns closely match the normal bell curve — this empirical observation is the justification for modelling returns as normally distributed random variables.

label: parameterised by
description: The normal distribution is fully described by its mean and variance — knowing these two parameters is enough to calculate any probability or quantile, including VaR.

label: increments of
description: Brownian motion is built from normal increments — each infinitesimal step dW is drawn from N(0, dt), so the normal distribution is the elementary building block of all continuous-time models.

label: underpins
description: Black-Scholes assumes lognormal prices (normal log returns), and the choice between normal and lognormal models determines whether the Black-Scholes or Bachelier formula applies.

label: contrasts
description: The comparison card explains why lognormal is the default for equity prices (guarantees positivity) but breaks down for interest rates that can turn negative.

label: contrasts
description: The comparison card explains when the normal distribution is appropriate (returns, rate models) versus when it fails (price levels that must remain positive).

label: implied by
description: GBM produces lognormal prices because it models proportional changes — the normal-vs-lognormal distinction is equivalent to choosing between arithmetic and geometric Brownian motion.

label: addresses
description: Jump-diffusion models add Poisson-distributed jumps to GBM to capture the fat tails and sudden crashes that pure Brownian motion cannot explain — a direct response to the limitations of the normal model.

label: augments
description: In Merton's jump-diffusion model, a Poisson process is layered on top of Brownian motion — the continuous diffusion handles normal price moves while Poisson jumps capture sudden large moves.

label: governs jumps in
description: The Poisson distribution governs the arrival of jumps in Merton's jump-diffusion model — the parameter λ sets the average number of jumps per year, and the memoryless property ensures jumps are unpredictable.

label: computes
description: The expected value of a continuous random variable is defined as the integral of x weighted by the PDF — E[X] = ∫x f(x) dx — making the density function the essential ingredient for computing fair values.

label: exemplified by
description: The normal distribution's bell-shaped PDF is the most widely used density in finance — its specific functional form determines the probabilities that feed into Black-Scholes, VaR, and every Gaussian risk model.

label: enables
description: The PIT maps each marginal variable to Uniform(0,1), separating marginal behaviour from dependence and enabling copula construction for multivariate risk models.

label: applies
description: The PIT applies the CDF F to a random variable X, producing F(X) ~ Uniform(0,1) — the fundamental link between any continuous distribution and the uniform.

label: foundation of
description: Inverse transform sampling is the direct application of the PIT in reverse: passing U ~ Uniform(0,1) through F_inverse generates draws from any target distribution.

label: used in
description: The Gaussian copula maps uniform marginals to normal variables via the probit function, a construction central to portfolio credit risk models and CDO pricing.

label: inverse of
description: The probit function Phi_inverse(p) is the functional inverse of the standardised normal CDF, converting probabilities back to z-scores.

label: determines
description: Parametric VaR at confidence level alpha is computed as mu + sigma * Phi_inverse(alpha), so the probit function directly sets the risk threshold.

label: inverse of
description: The quantile function is the mathematical inverse of the CDF — given a probability p, it returns the value x such that F(x) = p, reversing the direction of the CDF lookup.

label: powers
description: Inverse transform sampling generates random draws by passing uniform variates through the quantile function — Q(U) produces samples from any target distribution.

label: defines
description: Value at Risk is defined as a quantile of the loss distribution — VaR at confidence α is the negative of Q(1-α), making the quantile function the mathematical core of VaR computation.

label: used in
description: The Black-Scholes closed-form solution expresses the call and put prices as weighted combinations of Phi(d1) and Phi(d2), making the standardised normal function the computational core of option pricing.

label: quantile of
description: Parametric Value at Risk under normality equals mu + sigma * Phi_inverse(alpha), so the VaR threshold is computed directly from the inverse of the standardised normal function.

label: drives
description: Black-Scholes models the terminal stock price as S_T = S_0 exp((mu - sigma^2/2)T + sigma*sqrt(T)*Z), so a single standardised normal draw Z generates the entire price outcome.

label: distributed as
description: The CDF of the standardised normal variable Z is the function Phi(z) — knowing Z ~ N(0,1) means its cumulative probabilities are given by the standardised normal distribution function.

label: looked up in
description: Z-scores computed from a standardised normal variable are converted to cumulative probabilities by reading the Z-table, the manual counterpart to evaluating Phi(z).

label: sampled in
description: Monte Carlo simulation generates scenarios by sampling Z ~ N(0,1) and mapping each draw to asset prices via X = mu + sigma*Z, making the standardised normal variable the fundamental simulation input.

label: example of
description: The Student's t-distribution is the most commonly used fat-tailed distribution in finance — its polynomial tail decay and tuneable degrees-of-freedom parameter make it a tractable replacement for the normal when modelling heavy-tailed returns.

label: generalises
description: The t-distribution has heavier tails than the normal and converges to it as degrees of freedom increase — with low ν it captures the excess kurtosis observed in real asset returns.

label: improves
description: Using the t-distribution instead of the normal for VaR calculations produces wider tail quantiles, giving more conservative and realistic loss estimates that account for fat tails in returns.

label: complement of
description: The survival function S(x) = 1 - F(x) is the complement of the CDF — understanding the CDF-PDF relationship is prerequisite to understanding why S(x) decays as the CDF accumulates probability.

label: related via
description: The hazard rate equals f(x)/S(x) and the survival function equals exp(-∫h(t)dt) — the two functions are mathematically linked and jointly define the time-to-default dynamics in credit models.

label: ratio involving
description: Mill's ratio is the survival function divided by the PDF for the normal distribution — it provides a fast asymptotic approximation for the normal survival probability in the tails.

label: distinguishes
description: The tail dependence coefficient is the key property that distinguishes copula families — Gaussian copulas have zero tail dependence while Student-t and Clayton copulas have non-zero values, making copula selection a choice about tail behaviour.

label: critical for
description: Portfolio VaR in the tails depends on whether assets crash together — models with zero tail dependence systematically underestimate joint extreme losses, producing dangerously optimistic VaR at high confidence levels.

label: explains
description: Tail dependence — the tendency for extreme moves to coincide more often than normal correlation implies — is the statistical phenomenon that produces the correlation smile's upward slope at low strikes.

label: drives
description: Monte Carlo simulation begins with uniform U(0,1) random draws, which are transformed into the target distribution via the inverse CDF method — the uniform distribution is the raw input to every simulation.

label: estimates efficiently
description: All variance reduction techniques aim to estimate E[f(X)] with lower variance — control variates exploit the known expectation of a correlated quantity to subtract out estimation noise, whilst antithetic variates exploit symmetry of E[f(Z)] around zero.

label: improves
description: Variance reduction techniques — antithetic variates, control variates, importance sampling — are applied within Monte Carlo simulations to achieve the same pricing accuracy with far fewer paths, directly reducing the computational cost of derivatives pricing and risk measurement.

label: tabulates
description: The Z-table is a discrete tabulation of the standardised normal CDF Phi(z), providing pre-computed values at two-decimal-place precision.

label: provides quantiles for
description: Parametric VaR calculations require critical z-values (1.645 for 95%, 2.326 for 99%) that are read directly from the Z-table.

label: must incorporate
description: A realistic backtest must incorporate transaction cost analysis — strategies that appear profitable before costs can be worthless after accounting for spreads, market impact, and commissions.

label: inflated by
description: Repeatedly backtesting strategy variations on the same data inflates the probability of finding a spuriously significant result, making data snooping bias the primary threat to backtest credibility.

label: vulnerable to
description: Backtests are highly vulnerable to overfitting — a model with too many parameters will fit historical noise and produce impressive in-sample results that fail to generalise.

label: distorted by
description: Backtests conducted on survivor-only databases are systematically distorted upward, as they exclude the stocks that went bankrupt or were delisted — precisely the ones that would have caused losses.

label: defined via
description: Both VaR and Expected Shortfall are defined as functionals of the loss CDF — VaR is the quantile (inverse CDF) and ES is the average of quantiles beyond the VaR level, so the CDF is the mathematical foundation for evaluating coherence.

label: complements
description: EVT models the shape of the tail while coherence axioms govern how a risk measure aggregates tail losses — combining EVT-fitted tails with a coherent measure like Expected Shortfall produces robust extreme-risk estimates.

label: formalises
description: The coherence axioms formalise the shortcomings of naive risk measures — subadditivity failure in VaR is a specific, mathematically precise model limitation that coherent measures are designed to eliminate.

label: critiques
description: VaR violates the subadditivity axiom of coherence — merging portfolios can increase VaR, penalising diversification — which is the central motivation for replacing VaR with coherent alternatives like Expected Shortfall.

label: transferred to
description: Central clearing transfers bilateral counterparty credit risk to the CCP — instead of assessing each counterparty's creditworthiness, market participants face a single, regulated counterparty with a robust default waterfall.

label: mitigated by
description: Collateral management reduces counterparty credit risk by ensuring that positive exposures are continuously covered by posted collateral — if the counterparty defaults, the surviving party keeps the collateral.

label: extends
description: Counterparty credit risk extends VaR from market risk to credit risk — potential future exposure (PFE) uses the same simulation framework as VaR but measures the distribution of positive contract values conditional on counterparty default.

label: mitigated by
description: Central counterparties mitigate bilateral counterparty risk by interposing themselves between buyer and seller, guaranteeing performance through margining and default funds.

label: managed via
description: Counterparty risk is managed through collateral arrangements — posting initial and variation margin reduces exposure by ensuring that the non-defaulting party holds assets to offset potential losses.

label: concentrated in
description: For hedge funds, counterparty risk is heavily concentrated in the prime brokerage relationship — a prime broker failure can freeze assets, borrows, and margin financing simultaneously.

label: risk measure for
description: For absolute return strategies, drawdown duration measures how long the fund fails to deliver on its promise of positive returns — extended durations undermine investor confidence in the strategy's viability.

label: monitored by
description: Hedge fund investors track drawdown duration alongside depth — a prolonged drawdown often triggers investor redemptions regardless of its magnitude, creating a liquidity spiral that can force position liquidation.

label: complements
description: Maximum drawdown measures the depth of the worst loss while drawdown duration measures how long the recovery takes — together they capture both the severity and persistence of portfolio losses.

label: underpins IMA in
description: Under FRTB's Internal Models Approach, the headline capital metric is 97.5% Expected Shortfall over a stressed period — the SBM provides a standardised floor whilst IMA-approved desks replace it with their own ES-based model.

label: satisfies
description: Expected Shortfall is a coherent risk measure because it satisfies subadditivity — ES(A+B) ≤ ES(A) + ES(B) — which VaR does not; this mathematical property is what motivated Basel's FRTB to adopt 97.5% ES as the regulatory capital metric.

label: extends
description: Expected Shortfall is the average loss in the worst (1−α)% of scenarios, whereas VaR only marks the threshold — ES extends VaR by quantifying tail severity rather than tail probability, correcting VaR's inability to distinguish between thin-tailed and catastrophic loss distributions.

label: extends beyond
description: The normal distribution's exponentially decaying tails correspond to the Gumbel case (ξ = 0) of the GEV family — financial returns typically exhibit Fréchet-type tails (ξ > 0), which are heavier than the normal allows.

label: addresses
description: EVT directly confronts the fat-tail limitation of Gaussian risk models — by fitting only the extreme observations, it captures the heavy tails that the normal distribution misses.

label: improves tail of
description: EVT provides a statistically rigorous estimate of VaR at very high confidence levels (99.9%) by modelling the tail directly, rather than relying on the normal approximation that systematically underestimates extreme losses.

label: triggered by
description: Rising haircuts during market stress reduce the amount of leverage available, forcing leveraged investors to sell assets — the resulting fire sales push prices down, prompting lenders to raise haircuts further in a procyclical spiral.

label: manifestation of
description: Fire sales are the most damaging manifestation of liquidity risk — when market and funding liquidity dry up simultaneously, forced selling at distressed prices converts paper losses into permanent capital destruction.

label: caused by
description: Margin calls are the primary trigger of fire sales — when leveraged investors cannot meet margin demands with cash, they are forced to liquidate positions at whatever price the market will bear.

label: granular measure of
description: KRD provides the granular tenor-by-tenor sensitivity profile needed to assess banking book exposure under non-parallel yield curve scenarios prescribed by Basel's IRRBB framework.

label: primary tool for
description: Key rate duration is the primary tool for managing yield curve risk — by quantifying sensitivity at each tenor point, it enables targeted hedging of steepening, flattening, and twist scenarios.

label: decomposes
description: Key rate duration decomposes the single modified duration figure into tenor-specific sensitivities, revealing how interest rate exposure is distributed across the yield curve rather than assuming a parallel shift.

label: manifests via
description: Funding liquidity risk manifests when a firm cannot roll over its repos — lenders refuse to renew or demand higher haircuts, forcing the borrower to sell assets into a falling market.

label: amplifies
description: Liquidity risk amplifies the impact of margin calls — when forced to sell illiquid assets to meet margin demands, the resulting price impact can trigger further margin calls in a self-reinforcing spiral.

label: adjustment to
description: Standard VaR assumes positions can be unwound at mid-market prices, but liquidity-adjusted VaR adds the cost of crossing the bid-ask spread and market impact, producing a more realistic risk estimate.

label: constrains
description: Margin calls impose a hard limit on how much leverage an investor can sustain — when losses erode equity below the maintenance margin, the broker forces deleveraging through additional capital or position liquidation.

label: triggers at
description: Large drawdowns are the direct cause of margin calls — when cumulative losses push equity below the maintenance threshold, forced liquidation locks in the drawdown and can prevent recovery.

label: feeds into
description: Margin requirements are often calibrated using VaR — exchanges set initial margins to cover potential losses at a high confidence level (e.g. 99%), making VaR the mathematical foundation of margin policy.

label: caps
description: Initial margin ratios directly cap the maximum leverage a trader can employ — an initial margin of 10% limits leverage to 10x, regardless of the trader's risk appetite.

label: triggers
description: The maintenance margin threshold defines the exact point at which a margin call is issued — when equity falls below this floor, the broker demands additional collateral or liquidates positions.

label: calibrated by
description: Exchanges and clearing houses set initial margin levels to cover potential losses at a high confidence level (typically 99% VaR over the margin period of risk), making VaR the quantitative backbone of margin policy.

label: risk measure for
description: Maximum drawdown is a key risk metric for absolute return strategies — it captures the worst peak-to-trough loss and reveals tail risk that symmetric measures like standard deviation miss.

label: constrains
description: Hedge fund investors monitor maximum drawdown as a hard risk limit — breaching a drawdown threshold (e.g. 15–20%) often triggers investor redemptions or forced position liquidation.

label: complements
description: VaR estimates the worst expected loss over a single period at a given confidence level, while maximum drawdown captures the cumulative worst-case loss across multiple periods — together they provide a fuller picture of downside risk.

label: challenges
description: Real returns have fatter tails than the normal distribution predicts — extreme events like the 1987 crash occur far more often than the model allows, undermining VaR and Black-Scholes in the tails.

label: questions
description: The basic model assumes constant drift and volatility, but real markets exhibit volatility clustering and regime changes — motivating GARCH, stochastic volatility, and jump-diffusion models.

label: computes
description: Monte Carlo VaR simulates thousands of portfolio paths and reads the loss quantile directly from the resulting distribution, avoiding the normality assumption of parametric VaR.

label: implemented via
description: P&L attribution is implemented via the delta-gamma approximation — the Taylor expansion of portfolio value in terms of risk factor changes provides the decomposition into first- and second-order contributions.

label: decomposes using
description: P&L attribution decomposes daily profit and loss using the portfolio Greeks — delta, gamma, vega, theta, and rho each explain the P&L contribution from their respective risk factor movements.

label: validates
description: P&L attribution validates VaR models by comparing predicted risk factor sensitivities against actual P&L outcomes — persistent unexplained P&L suggests the risk model is missing important factors.

label: uses sensitivities from
description: Stress tests use the portfolio sensitivities (delta, gamma, vega) from the Greeks to estimate how the portfolio value changes under large moves in underlying risk factors.

label: informed by
description: Extreme value theory informs stress testing by providing a statistical framework for modelling the tails of loss distributions, helping calibrate the severity of hypothetical scenarios.

label: complements
description: Stress testing complements VaR by evaluating portfolio losses under extreme scenarios that VaR — which focuses on a specific confidence level under normal conditions — may not capture.

label: violates
description: Value at Risk is NOT a coherent risk measure because it can violate subadditivity — merging two portfolios can result in a VaR that is greater than the sum of the individual VaRs, discouraging diversification.

label: has
description: VaR's reliance on a single quantile of the loss distribution makes it blind to the severity of losses in the tail — this limitation means VaR can systematically underestimate the capital needed to survive extreme market events.

label: mitigates
description: Volatility scaling mitigates momentum crashes by automatically reducing exposure when volatility spikes — since crashes coincide with high-volatility regimes, the position is smallest when the risk is greatest.

label: improves risk-adjusted returns of
description: Volatility scaling nearly doubles the Sharpe ratio of momentum strategies by reducing exposure during high-volatility crash periods when momentum losses are most severe.

label: parameterised by
description: EWMA volatility is the most common estimator used to parameterise volatility scaling, as it reacts quickly to regime changes while remaining simple to compute.

label: exploits
description: Volatility scaling exploits volatility clustering — the empirical fact that high volatility persists — by using current volatility as a predictor of near-future volatility to size positions appropriately.

label: weaponises
description: A bear raid weaponises short selling by using aggressive, coordinated selling to artificially depress a stock price for profit, rather than simply expressing a bearish view.

label: can backfire into
description: A bear raid can backfire into a short squeeze if buyers absorb the selling pressure and push the price back up, trapping the raiders in losing short positions that they are forced to cover.

label: closes
description: Covering the short is the exit mechanism for a short sale — buying back borrowed shares to return them and realise the profit or loss on the position.

label: triggers
description: Mass covering by short sellers is the direct cause of a short squeeze — the collective buying pressure from forced or voluntary covering drives the price higher in a self-reinforcing loop.

label: derived from
description: Days to cover is calculated by dividing total short interest by average daily volume — short interest is the raw input that, when normalised by liquidity, yields the actionable squeeze-risk metric.

label: measures pressure of
description: Days to cover quantifies the aggregate exit risk for all short sellers in a stock — a high ratio means shorts collectively hold a position that would take many days of normal volume to unwind, amplifying directional pressure.

label: predicts
description: A high days-to-cover ratio is the primary quantitative signal for short squeeze risk — the more days required to cover, the more violent the forced buying when a positive catalyst arrives.

label: forces
description: Regulatory close-out requirements on fails-to-deliver force short sellers to cover their positions by purchasing shares in the open market within a specified timeframe.

label: indicates
description: Persistent fails-to-deliver are a key indicator of naked short selling, as they reveal that sellers did not have borrowed shares to deliver at settlement.

label: results from failure of
description: Fails-to-deliver often result from breakdowns in the securities lending chain — when a borrow is recalled or cannot be sourced, the short seller cannot deliver the shares at settlement.

label: prevents
description: The locate requirement is the regulatory mechanism that prevents naked short selling — by requiring a confirmed borrow before a short order is accepted, it ensures that every short sale has a real share behind it.

label: depends on
description: Obtaining a locate requires the prime broker to source shares from the securities lending market — without an active lending pool, locates cannot be granted and short selling capacity collapses.

label: prerequisite for
description: Under Regulation SHO, a broker must confirm a locate before executing any short sale — the locate requirement is the first mandatory step in the short-selling process and sets the capacity constraint for any short strategy.

label: determines cost of
description: When a locate is obtained for a hard-to-borrow security, the borrow rate charged reflects the scarcity of available shares — the locate process and the borrow rate are two sides of the same securities lending transaction.

label: triggered by
description: Margin calls arise directly from the use of leverage — when borrowed funds amplify a losing position, account equity can fall below the maintenance margin threshold, forcing the broker to issue a call for additional collateral.

label: forces
description: A margin call on a short position forces the trader to cover by buying back shares, as the broker requires the account to return to the minimum equity threshold.

label: risk of
description: Short sellers face margin calls when the shorted stock rises in price, reducing the equity in their margin account — because short losses are theoretically unlimited, margin call risk is a key constraint on position sizing.

label: can cause
description: Margin calls on short positions force involuntary buying, which pushes prices higher and triggers further margin calls in a feedback loop — this forced-covering cascade is a key mechanism driving short squeezes.

label: cost of
description: The margin lending rate is the direct cost of financial leverage — it determines the hurdle rate that a leveraged portfolio must exceed for the leverage to add value.

label: set by
description: The margin lending rate is set by the prime broker as a benchmark rate plus a spread, and is a key component of the overall cost of the prime brokerage relationship.

label: benchmarked to
description: The margin lending rate is typically quoted as the risk-free rate (or a proxy like SOFR) plus a credit spread, making changes in the risk-free rate the primary driver of margin borrowing costs.

label: compounds impact of
description: A higher margin lending rate increases the carrying cost of leveraged positions, compounding the financial pressure when a margin call forces the trader to either inject capital or liquidate.

label: violates
description: Naked short selling creates synthetic share supply without a genuine borrow, allowing prices to be artificially depressed below no-arbitrage levels — this breaks the assumption that short selling is limited to available lendable supply.

label: bypasses
description: Naked short selling is defined by the absence of a locate — the seller skips the step of confirming a borrowable share exists, making the locate requirement the direct regulatory counter to this practice.

label: unregulated variant of
description: Naked short selling is short selling without a confirmed borrow, in contrast to covered (standard) short selling where a locate is obtained first — it shares the same directional payoff but carries additional settlement and regulatory risk.

label: addresses
description: Regulation SHO addresses persistent fails-to-deliver by designating threshold securities and requiring mandatory close-out of open fail positions within 13 settlement days.

label: mandates
description: Regulation SHO mandates the locate requirement — broker-dealers must confirm that a security can be borrowed before accepting a short sale order.

label: restricts
description: Regulation SHO was specifically designed to restrict naked short selling through locate and close-out requirements that force delivery of borrowed shares.

label: codifies
description: Regulation SHO codifies the major short-sale constraints in US markets, including the locate rule, close-out requirements, and the alternative uptick rule (Rule 201).

label: triggers
description: A runaway short rapidly erodes account equity, triggering margin calls that force the trader to either inject capital or have the position forcibly closed at a loss.

label: worst case of
description: A runaway short is the worst-case outcome of a short sale, where the stock price rises persistently and the short seller faces theoretically unlimited losses.

label: exacerbated by
description: A runaway short is often exacerbated by short squeeze dynamics — as the price rises and short sellers are forced to cover, their buying pressure accelerates the very price increase causing their losses.

label: collateralised by
description: Securities lending requires the borrower to post collateral (typically 102–105% of the loan value), and this collateral is governed by the same margin and haircut frameworks used in leveraged trading.

label: enables
description: Securities lending provides the borrowed shares that short sellers need — without a functioning lending market, short selling and the price discovery it facilitates would be impossible.

label: measures
description: Short interest quantifies the aggregate volume of short selling in a stock — it is the primary metric for gauging how many investors are betting against a security.

label: predicts
description: Extremely high short interest (days to cover > 5, SI% > 20%) is the key predictor of short squeeze risk — the more shares sold short, the greater the forced buying pressure if prices rise.

label: drives
description: High short interest increases demand for borrowed shares, which pushes up the stock borrow rate — the two metrics are strongly correlated and together signal the difficulty and cost of maintaining a short position.

label: technique used in
description: A short ladder attack is a specific execution technique used within a bear raid — colluding parties trade back and forth at decreasing prices to create the illusion of selling pressure.

label: aims to cheapen
description: The goal of a short ladder attack is to depress the price so that the manipulators can cover their short positions at an artificially low price, maximising their profit.

label: manipulative form of
description: A short ladder attack is a manipulative application of short selling that uses wash trades between colluding parties to artificially depress a stock price.

label: violates
description: Short sale constraints prevent the execution of arbitrage trades that require shorting — without the ability to short, mispricings can persist because the no-arbitrage condition requires both buying underpriced and selling overpriced securities.

label: restricts
description: Short sale constraints limit the ability to sell short through uptick rules, bans, and locate requirements — these restrictions prevent pessimistic investors from expressing negative views and allow overpricing to persist.

label: drives up
description: Constraints on short selling reduce the supply of lendable shares, pushing up the stock borrow rate — the borrow cost is the market price of the constraint.

label: replicated by
description: Put-call parity shows that a short stock position can be synthetically replicated using options — buying a put and selling a call at the same strike creates an equivalent payoff without borrowing shares.

label: enables
description: Short selling is the fundamental tool that allows hedge funds to profit from overvalued securities and construct market-neutral portfolios — without it, hedging would be limited to derivatives.

label: inverts
description: Short selling inverts the standard return calculation — the seller profits when the asset price falls, creating a negative relationship between price movement and portfolio return.

label: triggers
description: Rising prices during a short squeeze erode short sellers' equity, triggering margin calls that force further buying — this margin-driven feedback loop is the mechanism that makes squeezes so violent.

label: amplified by
description: When lenders recall borrowed shares during a price spike, short sellers must buy back immediately — securities recalls amplify a squeeze by removing the option to hold the position.

label: risk of
description: A short squeeze is the primary tail risk of short selling — forced covering by short sellers creates a feedback loop that can drive prices far above fundamental value and generate unlimited losses.

label: priced by
description: The stock borrow rate is the price determined by supply and demand in the securities lending market — it reflects how scarce the stock is to borrow and how many short sellers are competing for available shares.

label: cost of
description: The borrow rate is the ongoing cost that makes short selling expensive for hard-to-borrow stocks — a trade with positive gross alpha can be unprofitable once the lending fee is deducted.

label: defends against
description: The uptick rule was specifically designed to defend against bear raids by preventing short sellers from continuously hitting the bid in a declining market.

label: type of
