description: The uptick rule is one of the primary short-sale constraints imposed by regulators — it restricts the price at which a short sale can be executed, adding a friction cost to short selling during declining markets.

label: constrains
description: The uptick rule constrains short selling by requiring that short orders be placed above the last transaction price or current best bid, preventing short sellers from relentlessly selling into a falling market at every downtick.

label: generalised by
description: GARCH extends ARCH by adding the lagged variance term βσ², which parsimoniously captures the long memory in volatility that ARCH needs many lagged squared-return terms to achieve.

label: formalises
description: ARCH was the first model to formalise volatility clustering mathematically — expressing conditional variance as a function of past squared shocks turned an empirical observation into a testable, estimable model.

label: corrects for
description: The 'augmented' part of the ADF test adds lagged difference terms to absorb serial correlation in the residuals — without these, the basic Dickey-Fuller test produces incorrect critical values.

label: tests for
description: The ADF test is applied to the residuals of a regression between two price series to test for cointegration — a stationary residual confirms a long-run equilibrium relationship suitable for pairs trading.

label: validates
description: The ADF test confirms that log-returns are stationary while log-prices are not — this validates the standard practice of modelling returns rather than prices in quantitative finance.

label: empirical basis for
description: Positive autocorrelation in asset returns at medium-term horizons (3–12 months) is the statistical foundation of momentum strategies — if returns were serially uncorrelated, momentum signals would have no predictive power.

label: exposes
description: Significant autocorrelation in squared returns reveals volatility clustering — a direct violation of the constant-volatility assumption in Black-Scholes and basic VaR models.

label: detected by
description: The ARCH-LM test checks for significant autocorrelation in squared returns — if present, it indicates volatility clustering and justifies the use of an ARCH/GARCH model.

label: addressed by
description: The ADF test includes lagged difference terms specifically to account for serial correlation in the residuals, ensuring that the test for a unit root is not biased by autocorrelation.

label: special case of
description: Autocorrelation is correlation applied to a time series and its own lagged values, rather than between two different variables — the same Pearson formula underpins both measures.

label: diagnoses
description: Testing for autocorrelation in returns is a key diagnostic — near-zero autocorrelation in raw returns supports market efficiency, while positive autocorrelation in squared returns confirms volatility clustering.

label: parameterises
description: Beta is the key input parameter to CAPM — estimating it from historical data is the essential step that turns the theoretical pricing equation into a practical tool for computing expected returns.

label: decomposed via
description: Beta can be decomposed as the product of the correlation between asset and market returns and the ratio of their standard deviations — separating the directional association from the relative volatility.

label: instance of
description: Estimating beta from a finite sample of returns is a parameter estimation problem — subject to sampling error, bias, and the choice of estimator (OLS, Bayesian shrinkage, or Blume adjustment).

label: normalised by
description: The OLS beta estimator divides the covariance of asset and market returns by the market variance — this normalisation converts a covariance into a unitless sensitivity measure.

label: alternative to
description: The CLT provides a fast alternative to inverse transform sampling: summing 12 uniform draws and subtracting 6 gives an approximate N(0,1) with mean 0, variance 1, but truncated tails.

label: proved via
description: The classic proof of the CLT uses moment generating functions — showing that the MGF of the standardised sum converges to the normal MGF establishes convergence in distribution to the Gaussian.

label: converges to
description: The CLT states that the standardised sum of i.i.d. random variables converges to the normal distribution — this is the fundamental reason the normal distribution is the default model for aggregate returns and portfolio losses.

label: justifies
description: Parametric VaR assumes portfolio losses are normally distributed — the CLT justifies this by guaranteeing that the sum of many independent risk factors is approximately normal, even when individual factors are not.

label: validates
description: The CLT guarantees that sample means are approximately normal for large n, which justifies constructing confidence intervals and hypothesis tests for estimated drift and volatility using normal quantiles.

label: validates
description: Cointegration testing is the statistical prerequisite for pairs trading — it confirms that two price series share a long-run equilibrium and that the spread is mean-reverting, not merely correlated.

label: distinct from
description: Correlation measures short-run co-movement while cointegration captures long-run equilibrium — two series can be highly correlated without being cointegrated, or cointegrated with low short-term correlation.

label: implies
description: Cointegration implies that the linear combination of two non-stationary series is stationary and mean-reverting — it provides the rigorous statistical basis for the mean-reversion assumption in spread trading.

label: relies on
description: Confidence interval formulas assume the sampling distribution of the mean is approximately normal — a result provided by the Central Limit Theorem, which justifies using z or t critical values.

label: quantifies uncertainty of
description: Confidence intervals wrap around point estimates of drift and volatility to show how precisely they have been measured — wide intervals signal that the estimate may be unreliable for pricing or risk calculations.

label: requires
description: The width of a confidence interval is directly proportional to the standard deviation — higher variability in the data produces wider intervals and less precise estimates of the population parameter.

label: feeds into
description: Portfolio VaR depends on the correlations between positions — in a multi-asset portfolio, low correlations reduce aggregate risk even when individual volatilities are unchanged.

label: assembled into
description: Each pairwise covariance becomes an entry in the covariance matrix — the matrix form enables the compact expression wᵀΣw for portfolio variance and unlocks linear algebra tools like eigendecomposition and Cholesky factorisation.

label: feeds into
description: The covariance matrix is the central input to Markowitz optimisation — portfolio variance is wᵀΣw, so every allocation decision is ultimately driven by the pairwise covariances between asset returns.

label: normalised to
description: Correlation is covariance divided by the product of the two standard deviations — this normalisation removes scale dependence and produces a dimensionless measure bounded between −1 and +1.

label: modelled by
description: The covariance between two asset returns in continuous time is modelled via correlated Brownian motions — the instantaneous covariance ρσ₁σ₂ dt drives multi-asset pricing and hedging.

label: invalidates naive
description: Data snooping invalidates naive hypothesis testing — when many hypotheses are tested on the same data, the standard p-value threshold of 5% dramatically understates the true false discovery rate.

label: corrected by
description: Data snooping bias is corrected by multiple testing adjustments such as the Bonferroni correction and false discovery rate control, which raise the significance threshold to account for the number of tests performed.

label: extends
description: EGARCH extends the ARCH family by modelling the log-variance with an asymmetric response to positive and negative shocks — capturing the leverage effect that symmetric ARCH and GARCH models miss.

label: asymmetric variant of
description: EGARCH adds a signed shock term to GARCH, allowing negative returns to increase volatility more than positive returns of the same magnitude — matching the empirically observed leverage effect in equity markets.

label: explains
description: The leverage effect captured by EGARCH — negative returns increasing volatility disproportionately — is a primary driver of the implied volatility skew observed in equity options.

label: calibrates
description: The annualised drift and volatility used in every pricing and risk model are estimated from historical daily returns — multiplying the mean by 252 and the standard deviation by √252.

label: feeds into
description: The RiskMetrics EWMA model was the industry standard for computing conditional VaR through the 1990s — its adaptive volatility estimate replaced the static historical window with an exponentially weighted one.

label: special case of
description: EWMA is a restricted GARCH(1,1) where α + β = 1 and ω = 0 — this removes mean reversion, making volatility shocks persist indefinitely rather than decaying to a long-run level.

label: approximates
description: A factor model approximates the full covariance matrix as BΣ₆Bᵀ + D, reducing the number of parameters from O(n²) to O(n) and producing a better-conditioned estimate for large portfolios.

label: generalises
description: The Fama-French model is a specific three-factor model — the general factor model framework extends to any number of fundamental, macroeconomic, or statistical factors.

label: estimated via
description: Statistical factor models use PCA to extract the dominant eigenvectors of the covariance matrix as factors — the eigenvalues determine how many factors to retain.

label: improves
description: GARCH-based conditional volatility produces VaR estimates that adapt to current market conditions — during turbulent periods the forecast widens automatically, unlike static historical volatility.

label: models
description: GARCH is the standard parametric model for volatility clustering — the α and β coefficients directly capture how past shocks and past variance feed into today's conditional variance.

label: framework for
description: The ADF test is a specific application of hypothesis testing — it tests the null hypothesis that a time series has a unit root, using the standard framework of test statistic, critical values, and rejection regions.

label: relies on
description: Most classical hypothesis tests assume the test statistic is approximately normally distributed under the null — a result guaranteed by the Central Limit Theorem when sample sizes are large enough.

label: dual of
description: A hypothesis test at significance level α rejects the null if and only if the hypothesised value falls outside the corresponding (1 − α) confidence interval — the two approaches are mathematically equivalent.

label: framework for
description: The Jarque-Bera test is a specific hypothesis test for normality — it applies the general hypothesis testing framework to check whether sample skewness and kurtosis are consistent with a normal distribution.

label: specialised as
description: The t-test is the most widely used specialisation of hypothesis testing — it adapts the general framework to the common case where the population standard deviation is unknown and must be estimated from the sample.

label: quantified by
description: Type I and Type II errors are the two ways a hypothesis test can go wrong — the significance level α controls the Type I rate, while power analysis addresses the Type II rate, making them the core performance metrics of any test.

label: tests for
description: The Jarque-Bera test is a formal hypothesis test for normality — it checks whether a sample's skewness and kurtosis are consistent with the normal distribution, which is the assumed return distribution in many pricing and risk models.

label: diagnoses
description: When the Jarque-Bera test rejects normality for a return series, it provides direct statistical evidence that Gaussian-based models (Black-Scholes, parametric VaR) may understate tail risk — motivating fat-tailed alternatives.

label: formalises
description: The Jarque-Bera test converts the descriptive observation that returns exhibit skewness and excess kurtosis into a formal statistical test with a p-value, quantifying how far the data deviate from normality.

label: estimates hedge ratio for
description: The Kalman filter estimates time-varying hedge ratios in pairs trading, avoiding the lookback-window problem of rolling OLS by updating the estimate incrementally with each new observation.

label: requires
description: The Kalman gain computation requires matrix inversion of the innovation covariance (HPH^T + R), making efficient matrix algebra essential for real-time Kalman filter implementations.

label: dynamic version of
description: The Kalman filter provides a dynamic, time-varying version of beta estimation — instead of a fixed regression coefficient, beta is modelled as a hidden state that evolves over time.

label: estimates
description: KDE produces a non-parametric estimate of the PDF — understanding what a PDF is and how it relates to the CDF is prerequisite to interpreting KDE output.

label: uses as kernel
description: The Gaussian kernel used in KDE is the standard normal PDF — each data point gets a normal bump of width h, and summing all bumps produces the density estimate.

label: reveals
description: KDE applied to return data reveals the empirical density shape — fat tails, skewness, and multimodality that parametric models would miss — providing a visual diagnostic of the statistical properties of returns.

label: benchmarked against
description: The normal distribution serves as the reference point for kurtosis — its kurtosis of exactly 3 (excess kurtosis 0) is the baseline, and deviations from this value indicate heavier or lighter tails than the Gaussian model assumes.

label: feeds into
description: High kurtosis means extreme losses occur more frequently than a normal model predicts — VaR estimates that ignore excess kurtosis systematically understate tail risk, which is why practitioners adjust for fat tails when calibrating loss distributions.

label: explains
description: The volatility smile arises because the market prices in fatter tails than Black-Scholes assumes — kurtosis quantifies exactly this tail heaviness, and higher excess kurtosis in the risk-neutral distribution produces a more pronounced smile.

label: requires
description: Computing the Mahalanobis distance requires inverting the covariance matrix to account for correlations and differing scales across variables.

label: distributed as
description: For multivariate normal data, the squared Mahalanobis distance follows a chi-squared distribution with p degrees of freedom, enabling formal outlier tests.

label: generalises
description: The Mahalanobis distance is the multivariate generalisation of the z-score — it measures how many standard deviations a vector lies from the mean, accounting for correlations.

label: constrains
description: When many parameter estimates are tested for significance, multiple testing correction raises the bar for declaring any single estimate statistically significant — preventing data-mined parameter choices from appearing artificially robust.

label: extends
description: Multiple testing correction extends the single-test framework to scenarios where many hypotheses are tested simultaneously — it adjusts significance thresholds to control the overall false positive rate across all tests.

label: controls
description: Multiple testing corrections directly control the aggregate Type I error rate when many tests are performed — Bonferroni controls the family-wise error rate while Benjamini–Hochberg controls the false discovery rate.

label: computed via
description: The OLS estimator β̂ = (XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy requires inverting the Gram matrix XᵀX — matrix inversion is the core linear algebra operation in regression.

label: calibrates
description: OLS regression is the standard method for estimating CAPM betas from historical return data — regressing excess asset returns on excess market returns yields the beta coefficient.

label: foundation of
description: Beta estimation in CAPM and factor models is a direct application of OLS regression — the slope coefficient from regressing asset returns on market returns is the asset's beta.

label: related to
description: Overfitting and data snooping are related but distinct: overfitting concerns model complexity (too many parameters), while data snooping concerns testing multiplicity (too many hypotheses) — both produce spurious results.

label: undermines
description: Overfitting undermines hypothesis testing by producing artificially low p-values in sample — the model has memorised the data rather than discovered a genuine relationship, so standard significance tests are unreliable.

label: objective of
description: Markowitz optimisation minimises portfolio variance for a given target return — the portfolio variance formula is the objective function that defines every point on the efficient frontier.

label: built from
description: Portfolio variance depends on pairwise covariances between all assets — the cross-terms 2w₁w₂Cov(R₁,R₂) are what make diversification reduce total risk below the weighted sum of individual risks.

label: models
description: PCA applied to yield curve movements typically reveals that three factors (level, slope, and curvature) explain over 95% of the variance, providing a parsimonious model for interest rate risk.

label: decomposes
description: PCA is the eigendecomposition of the covariance matrix — it identifies the orthogonal directions of greatest variance, revealing the dominant risk factors hidden in the correlation structure.

label: applies
description: Each principal component is an eigenvector of the covariance matrix, and its eigenvalue measures the variance explained — PCA is the most important practical application of eigendecomposition in finance.

label: motivates use of
description: When a Q-Q plot reveals non-normality, the Cornish-Fisher expansion provides a quantile correction that accounts for the observed skewness and kurtosis.

label: detects
description: An S-shaped departure from the reference line in a Q-Q plot is the signature of fat tails, revealing that the data have heavier extremes than the normal distribution predicts.

label: compares against
description: A normal Q-Q plot compares sample quantiles against theoretical quantiles of Z ~ N(0,1), using the standardised normal variable as the reference distribution.

label: detects onset of
description: Regime switching models can detect the onset of conditions associated with momentum crashes — a transition to the high-volatility bear regime is the environment in which momentum reversals are most likely.

label: informs
description: Regime detection informs volatility scaling decisions — when the model signals a transition to the high-volatility regime, exposure can be reduced before the full impact of the regime change materialises.

label: explains
description: Regime switching models explain volatility clustering as the market alternating between a low-volatility and high-volatility regime, with each regime having its own persistent volatility level.

label: contrasted in
description: Mean returns scale linearly with time while volatility scales with the square root — this asymmetry explains why drift dominates at long horizons but is invisible at short ones.

label: regularises
description: Shrinkage blends the noisy sample covariance matrix with a structured target to produce a better-conditioned estimate — essential when the number of assets approaches the sample size.

label: improves
description: Shrinkage reduces estimation error by trading a small amount of bias for a large reduction in variance — the classic bias-variance tradeoff applied to covariance estimation.

label: absent from
description: The normal distribution has zero skewness by symmetry — any observed skewness in return data is direct evidence that the Gaussian assumption is inadequate for tail risk estimation.

label: characterises
description: Skewness is one of the key stylised facts of financial returns — equity indices typically show negative skewness, meaning crash risk is more pronounced than a symmetric distribution would imply.

label: explains
description: Negative skewness in equity returns is the physical-measure counterpart of the implied volatility skew — the market prices out-of-the-money puts higher because large downward moves are more probable than a symmetric model predicts.

label: scales
description: The square-root rule is used to convert VaR between time horizons — a 1-day VaR is scaled to a 10-day VaR by multiplying by √10, as required by Basel regulatory standards.

label: explains
description: Because volatility grows as √t while drift grows as t, the signal-to-noise ratio improves with horizon length — the square-root rule is the quantitative reason why short-term forecasting is so difficult.

label: motivates
description: The empirically observed fat tails, excess kurtosis, and occasional extreme moves in financial returns are precisely the phenomena that EVT is designed to model — stylised facts of returns provide the motivation for using EVT.

label: motivates
description: Empirical return distributions exhibit fat tails, skewness, and volatility clustering that deviate from the normal assumption — these observed properties are the primary evidence motivating more sophisticated risk models.

label: interacts with
description: Survivorship bias interacts with data snooping: researchers testing many strategies on a biased dataset face both the inflated returns of survivor-only data and the multiple-testing problem simultaneously.

label: compounds
description: Survivorship bias compounds overfitting by inflating the apparent performance of backtested strategies — a model fit to survivor-only data is optimised against an already-biased sample.

label: validates
description: The t-test is used to determine whether an estimated beta coefficient is statistically different from zero — the t-statistic reported alongside regression coefficients is the ratio of the estimate to its standard error.

label: requires
description: The t-test uses the sample standard deviation as an estimate of the unknown population standard deviation — this substitution is what introduces the extra uncertainty captured by the t-distribution rather than the normal.

label: relies on
description: Power and error probability calculations assume the test statistic is normally distributed under both the null and alternative hypotheses — an approximation justified by the Central Limit Theorem for large samples.

label: constrains
description: The precision of estimated parameters (drift and volatility) determines the power of hypothesis tests — noisy estimates widen confidence intervals and increase the probability of Type II errors when testing whether a strategy has genuine alpha.

label: extended by
description: Variance measures dispersion at a single point in time, while autocorrelation extends this to measure how dispersion persists across time — connecting static risk measurement to dynamic volatility behaviour.

label: special case of
description: Variance is the covariance of a variable with itself — Cov(X, X) = Var(X) — making covariance the natural generalisation of variance to pairs of variables.

label: foundation of
description: Kurtosis is defined as the fourth moment divided by the square of the variance — without understanding variance as the second central moment, the standardisation that makes kurtosis comparable across distributions is meaningless.

label: extended by
description: Variance captures the spread of a distribution (second moment) while skewness captures its asymmetry (third moment) — both are needed to fully describe the shape of a return distribution beyond its centre.

label: underpins
description: The square-root rule follows directly from the variance addition property for independent random variables — total variance equals the sum of individual variances, so total standard deviation grows as √n.

label: invalidates assumptions of
description: Volatility clustering means that the constant-variance assumption behind parametric VaR is violated — during turbulent periods the true conditional volatility is much higher than the unconditional estimate, causing VaR to understate risk precisely when it matters most.

label: motivates
description: The empirical observation that large return shocks tend to be followed by more large shocks is the stylised fact that GARCH models were specifically designed to capture.

label: refines
description: While the statistical properties card establishes that standardised returns are approximately normal, volatility clustering reveals that the variance used for standardisation is itself time-varying — a deeper stylised fact that simple summary statistics miss.

label: extends
description: Variance and standard deviation treat volatility as a fixed number, but volatility clustering shows that it changes over time — motivating conditional variance models like GARCH that update the volatility estimate as new data arrives.

label: motivates
description: The empirical observation that volatility clusters in time is the primary motivation for stochastic volatility models like Heston — the model's mean-reverting variance process directly captures the persistence of high- and low-volatility regimes.

label: shapes dynamics of
description: Volatility clustering means realised volatility is autocorrelated — periods of high realised vol tend to persist, which any practical estimator or trading strategy must account for.

label: foundation of
description: The backward Kolmogorov equation is the skeleton of the Black-Scholes PDE — adding risk-neutral drift rS dV/dS and discounting -rV to the pure diffusion equation yields the full Black-Scholes equation.

label: driven by
description: The diffusion coefficient c enters the backward Kolmogorov equation as the c² term multiplying the second spatial derivative — it controls the rate at which uncertainty in the terminal payoff propagates backward in time.

label: adjoint of
description: The backward Kolmogorov equation and the Fokker-Planck (forward Kolmogorov) equation are adjoint PDEs — the forward equation evolves the probability density forward in time, while the backward equation evolves expected payoffs backward from a terminal condition.

label: component of
description: The Brownian increment dW ~ N(0, dt) is the infinitesimal building block of Brownian motion — the process is constructed from its independent, normally distributed increments.

label: drives
description: The Brownian increment dW is what drives the diffusion term σ S dW in an SDE — its sqrt(dt) scaling means the random component dominates the deterministic drift at short time scales.

label: foundation of
description: Because dW is of order sqrt(dt), its square dW² is of order dt and cannot be ignored — this is the direct cause of the rule dW² = dt.

label: underpins
description: The Bachelier model assumes the forward price follows arithmetic Brownian motion — the simplest continuous-time stochastic process — making it the most direct application of Brownian motion to option pricing.

label: underlies
description: Because the underlying asset follows a Brownian-driven process, option prices are stochastic and their sensitivities (the Greeks) describe how that randomness flows through to the portfolio.

label: extended to
description: Geometric Brownian Motion replaces the additive increments of standard Brownian Motion with multiplicative ones, ensuring the stock price can never go negative. Brownian motion supplies the dW noise term in the GBM stochastic differential equation dS = μS dt + σS dW — it is the mechanical source of randomness, distinct from the mathematical generalisation that GBM represents.

label: example of
description: Standard Brownian motion is the canonical continuous-time martingale — its independent, zero-mean increments ensure that the conditional expectation of any future value equals the current value.

label: integrator of
description: Stochastic integrals (Itô integrals) are defined with respect to Brownian motion paths — the integrator's non-zero quadratic variation is what distinguishes them from Riemann integrals.

label: built on
description: The compound Poisson process uses a Poisson-distributed count N(t) to determine how many random jumps have occurred by time t — the Poisson distribution governs the arrival times while the jump sizes are drawn from a separate distribution.

label: contrasts with
description: Brownian motion produces continuous sample paths while the compound Poisson process produces pure-jump paths — together they form the two fundamental building blocks of Levy processes used in finance.

label: jump component of
description: The compound Poisson process supplies the jump term in Merton's jump-diffusion model — the continuous diffusion handles normal market moves while the compound Poisson component captures sudden large price changes.

label: uses
description: The correlation coefficient rho parameterises how two Brownian motions move together, directly importing the statistical concept of correlation into stochastic calculus.

label: extends
description: Correlated Brownian motions extend single Brownian motion to multiple linked processes with E[dW1 dW2] = rho dt, enabling multi-asset modelling.

label: required for
description: Correlated Brownian motions produce the cross term dS1 dS2 = rho sigma1 sigma2 S1 S2 dt that appears in the multi-asset Ito s lemma.

label: state-dependent
description: The CIR diffusion sigma*sqrt(r) depends on the current rate level — as r approaches zero the noise vanishes, preventing negative rates when the Feller condition is met.

label: contrasts with
description: Both CIR and GBM have level-dependent diffusion, but CIR uses sqrt(r) (keeping rates non-negative) while GBM uses S (keeping prices positive), and CIR is mean-reverting while GBM is not.

label: example of
description: The CIR model is a mean-reverting short-rate model with the same drift structure gamma(r_bar - r) as the Vasicek model but with level-dependent volatility sigma*sqrt(r).

label: appears in
description: The diffusion coefficient σS enters the Black-Scholes PDE as the ½σ²S² term multiplying the second derivative — it is the source of the Gamma risk that delta hedging must manage.

label: multiplies
description: The diffusion coefficient scales the Brownian increment dW — without it, the raw Wiener process has unit variance per unit time, so the diffusion coefficient is what calibrates the noise to match observed market volatility.

label: scales by
description: The diffusion coefficient σS combines the volatility parameter σ with the current price S — volatility sets the percentage scale of randomness, while the diffusion coefficient converts it into absolute price units.

label: implements
description: Monte Carlo simulation works by repeatedly applying the discrete random walk formula with fresh random draws — each application of the formula generates one step of one simulated price path.

label: becomes
description: The discrete random walk is the finite-step approximation that becomes the continuous SDE dS = μS dt + σS dX as the time step δt → 0.

label: replaced under
description: Under the risk-neutral measure the drift coefficient changes from μS to rS — this replacement is the mathematical content of risk-neutral pricing and the reason option prices do not depend on expected returns.

label: paired with
description: The drift and diffusion coefficients are the two components of any SDE — the drift controls the deterministic trend while the diffusion controls the random fluctuations, and together they fully specify the process.

label: formalises
description: The drift coefficient a(S) = μS is the continuous-time formalisation of the drift parameter μ — it converts the annualised expected return into an instantaneous rate of price change proportional to the current level.

label: modifies
description: In ordinary calculus, the second-order Taylor term dx² vanishes; the rule dW² = dt means the stochastic second-order term survives, fundamentally changing how Taylor expansions work for random processes.

label: foundation of
description: The rule dW² = dt is the key substitution in deriving Itô’s lemma — it converts the second-order Taylor term ½ f′′ dW² into the deterministic Itô correction ½ f′′ dt.

label: foundation of
description: The equivalence of the physical and risk-neutral measures is the mathematical expression of no-arbitrage — the First Fundamental Theorem states that a market is arbitrage-free iff an equivalent martingale measure exists.

label: quantified by
description: The Radon–Nikodým derivative is the density function that formalises the relationship between equivalent measures — it converts expectations under one measure to expectations under the other.

label: enables
description: Euler-Maruyama discretisation is the core engine of Monte Carlo simulation for SDEs — it provides the time-stepping rule that generates each sample path.

label: applied to
description: Euler-Maruyama applied to GBM gives S_{i+1} = S_i(1 + mu*dt + sigma*phi*sqrt(dt)), though GBM also has an exact exponential formula that avoids discretisation error.

label: applied to
description: Euler-Maruyama applied to the Vasicek model gives r_{i+1} = r_i + gamma(r_bar - r_i)*dt + sigma*phi*sqrt(dt), the standard recipe for simulating mean-reverting interest rates.

label: discretises
description: The Euler-Maruyama method converts a continuous-time SDE into a discrete stepping rule by replacing dt with delta t and dW with phi*sqrt(delta t), enabling computer simulation of any SDE.

label: derived via
description: The drift condition f = −½g² for exponential martingales is found by applying Itô’s lemma to e^Y and setting the resulting drift coefficient to zero.

label: special case of
description: Exponential martingales are a specific class of positive martingales constructed by exponentiating a process with carefully chosen drift — they serve as Radon–Nikodým derivatives for measure changes.

label: solution of
description: The Black-Scholes PDE is solved by the Feynman–Kac formula, which equates the PDE boundary-value problem to a risk-neutral expectation of the discounted payoff — this is why Monte Carlo and analytic pricing formulas give the same answer.

label: formalises
description: Feynman–Kac provides the mathematical justification for risk-neutral pricing: it proves that the risk-neutral expectation formula and the Black-Scholes PDE are two equivalent representations of the same derivative price.

label: generalises
description: The backward Kolmogorov equation is the special case of Feynman–Kac with no discounting and no running cost — Feynman–Kac adds a discount rate r·u and source term to yield the full result used in derivative pricing.

label: derived via
description: Feynman–Kac is proved by applying Itô's lemma to the candidate solution u(t, X_t), which shows that u satisfies the required PDE if and only if the resulting stochastic integral is a martingale.

label: requires
description: The Fokker-Planck (forward Kolmogorov) equation requires an initial condition — typically a delta function at the starting point — to select the unique transition probability density from its infinitely many solutions.

label: dual of
description: The Fokker-Planck equation and the Black-Scholes PDE are adjoint operators — Fokker-Planck evolves the density forward in time while Black-Scholes evolves the option price backward, both governed by the same drift and diffusion.

label: derives
description: Solving the Fokker-Planck equation for GBM confirms that the terminal stock price is lognormally distributed — the equation provides the rigorous proof of the distributional assumption underlying Black-Scholes.

label: solves for
description: The Fokker-Planck equation evolves the entire probability density function over time — the VaR quantile can then be read directly from the resulting terminal distribution.

label: driven by
description: The diffusion coefficient b(X) enters the Fokker-Planck equation as the second-order term ½∂²(b²p)/∂x² — it determines how quickly the probability density spreads out over time.

label: driven by
description: The drift coefficient a(X) enters the Fokker-Planck equation as the first-order term −∂(ap)/∂x — it determines the direction in which the probability density shifts over time.

label: specialises
description: The Fokker-Planck steady state is the time-independent solution of the Fokker-Planck equation, obtained by setting the time derivative to zero for a mean-reverting process.

label: recovers
description: The inverse Fokker-Planck problem recovers the drift function from an observed steady-state density — a model-free calibration approach.

label: characterises
description: The steady-state density exists only for mean-reverting processes — it characterises the long-run distribution that rates or volatilities fluctuate around.

label: is a
description: The Gaussian transition density is a normal distribution whose mean is the starting position and whose variance grows linearly with the time interval — the central limit theorem guarantees this Gaussian shape for any diffusion built from independent increments.

label: exhibits
description: The standard deviation of the transition density scales as the square root of elapsed time — the same √t scaling that governs how volatility converts between time horizons in finance.

label: characterises
description: The Gaussian transition density completely characterises Brownian motion — it gives the exact probability of the process reaching any point at any future time, encoding the independent Gaussian increments property.

label: solves
description: The Gaussian transition density is the fundamental solution (Green's function) of the Fokker-Planck equation with zero drift and constant diffusion — it satisfies the PDE with a delta-function initial condition.

label: models stock price for
description: Black-Scholes assumes the stock price follows GBM — this single assumption leads directly to the lognormal distribution of returns and the closed-form pricing formula.

label: emerges from
description: Geometric Brownian Motion is the continuous-time stock-price model that the binomial tree converges to in the limit — the CRR up/down parameters are chosen precisely so that this convergence holds.

label: models dynamics of
description: Geometric Brownian motion is the standard model for the dynamics of the underlying price in the Black-Scholes framework, ensuring that S remains positive and returns are lognormally distributed.

label: produces
description: GBM implies that log-prices are normally distributed, so the price itself follows a lognormal distribution — this is why asset prices are always positive and right-skewed. The analytical solution of the GBM SDE is S_T = S₀ exp((μ − ½σ²)T + σW_T), which is a lognormal random variable — the lognormal distribution provides both the closed-form solution and the transition density needed for option pricing.

label: models returns for
description: GBM provides the analytical basis for parametric VaR — because it implies log-returns are normally distributed, the loss quantile can be computed directly from the volatility parameter.

label: extended by
description: Jump-diffusion models extend GBM by adding a Poisson-driven jump component to the continuous diffusion — GBM is recovered as the special case where jump intensity λ = 0.

label: becomes under Q
description: Under the real-world measure, GBM with drift μ ≠ r is not a martingale after discounting — but under the risk-neutral measure Q, the discounted GBM stock price becomes a martingale, which is the key insight enabling derivative pricing.

label: enables
description: Girsanov’s theorem replaces the real-world drift μ with the risk-free rate r, which is why the Black–Scholes formula does not depend on μ — the drift is absorbed into the definition of Brownian motion under Q.

label: transforms
description: Girsanov’s theorem shows how a Brownian motion with drift under one measure becomes a standard driftless Brownian motion under an equivalent measure — the drift is absorbed into the measure change.

label: computed via
description: Itô’s lemma produces the SDE for a function of a stochastic process, separating it into dt (drift) and dW (Itô integral) components — the Itô integral part vanishes under expectation.

label: produces
description: Every Itô integral is a martingale with zero expectation — this is the fundamental property that makes stochastic integrals with respect to Brownian motion vanish when taking expectations.

label: relies on
description: The Itô isometry holds because Brownian motion has independent increments with variance dt — cross terms vanish when squaring the stochastic integral, leaving only the diagonal terms.

label: computes variance of
description: The Itô isometry converts the expected square of a stochastic integral into an ordinary integral of the squared integrand — the primary tool for computing variances of Itô integrals.

label: applies to
description: When two correlated Brownian motions are multiplied, the Itô product rule generates a drift term ρ dt from the cross-variation — this drives correlation risk in multi-asset derivative pricing.

label: extends
description: The Itô product rule extends Itô’s lemma to products of stochastic processes — the extra quadratic covariation term dX dY arises from the same second-order effects that produce the Itô correction.

label: derives
description: Applying Itô's Lemma to the option price as a function of a GBM stock price, then invoking the no-arbitrage condition, yields the Black-Scholes PDE.

label: solves
description: Itô's Lemma is used to derive the explicit solution for GBM — applying it to ln(S) converts the stochastic differential equation into a simple integral that gives S(t) in closed form. Applying Itô’s Lemma to ln(S) reveals the −½σ² convexity correction in the drift of log-returns — this correction is why E[S_T] = S₀ e^{μT} but the median is S₀ e^{(μ−½σ²)T}, a distinction central to risk management.

label: derives
description: Applying Ito s lemma to ln S under GBM produces the -sigma^2/2 correction in the drift — this is exactly the volatility drag, showing that compound growth is lower than the arithmetic mean return.

label: applied in
description: Ito s lemma is applied to g(S) and the drift terms are set to zero, producing the ODE B g  + (1/2) A^2 g   = 0 that defines the zero-drift condition.

label: applies to
description: Itô's lemma is the fundamental tool for differentiating functions of the Heston stochastic processes, enabling the derivation of the model's characteristic function and its pricing PDE.

label: derives
description: The exponential example e^{W_t} picking up a +½ dt drift is the origin of the -½σ² correction in the GBM log-return formula — connecting abstract Itô calculus to concrete asset pricing.

label: applies
description: The worked examples apply Itô’s lemma mechanically to power, exponential, logarithmic, and trigonometric functions of Brownian motion — building fluency with the formula.

label: explains
description: Jump-diffusion models produce a volatility smile because the possibility of sudden downward jumps increases the value of out-of-the-money puts relative to Black-Scholes, raising their implied volatility and creating the characteristic smile shape across strikes.

label: motivated by
description: Fat-tailed distributions in empirical asset returns motivate Lévy processes — by including a jump component via the Lévy measure, these processes naturally generate heavier tails than the Gaussian distribution produced by pure Brownian motion.

label: contains as special case
description: A compound Poisson process is a Lévy process whose Lévy measure has finite total mass — the number of jumps in any interval is Poisson-distributed, making it the simplest pure-jump Lévy process.

label: generalises
description: Brownian motion is the unique continuous Lévy process — it corresponds to the special case where the Lévy measure is zero and only the Gaussian component remains in the Lévy–Khintchine triplet.

label: foundation of
description: Jump-diffusion models such as Merton’s model are Lévy processes that combine a continuous Brownian component with a compound Poisson jump component — the Lévy–Khintchine triplet has both non-zero sigma and a finite Lévy measure.

label: characterises
description: Lévy’s characterisation provides an equivalent definition of Brownian motion using martingale conditions rather than independent normally distributed increments — a more natural formulation for stochastic calculus.

label: applies
description: Lévy’s characterisation defines Brownian motion through two martingale conditions: X_t is a martingale and X_t² − t is a martingale, connecting the theory of martingales directly to the fundamental stochastic process.

label: satisfied by
description: Standard Brownian motion is both Markov and a martingale — its future depends only on the current value (Markov) and its conditional expectation equals the current value (martingale).

label: independent of
description: The Markov property (memoryless) and the martingale property (driftless) are logically independent — a process can be one, both, or neither, and confusing them is a common error.

label: guarantees hedging for
description: The MRT guarantees that every contingent claim in a complete market has a replicating portfolio — the integrand in the representation is the delta hedging strategy, which is the mathematical foundation of dynamic hedging.

label: converse of
description: The MRT is the converse of the fact that Itô integrals are martingales: not only does every Itô integral produce a martingale, but every Brownian martingale can be represented as an Itô integral.

label: applied in
description: The mathematical martingale definition (conditional expectation equals current value) is applied across the quant finance industry for pricing, hedging, model validation, and no-arbitrage enforcement.

label: formalises
description: The no-arbitrage principle is equivalent to the existence of an equivalent martingale measure — if no arbitrage exists, then there is a probability measure under which all discounted asset prices are martingales.

label: foundation of
description: Risk-neutral pricing rests on the martingale property — under the risk-neutral measure Q, discounted asset prices are martingales, which is the mathematical condition that guarantees no-arbitrage pricing.

label: defined via
description: The martingale property is a statement about conditional expected values — E[M_t | F_s] = M_s — so understanding expectation is a prerequisite for understanding martingales.

label: models rates for
description: Mean-reverting models like Vasicek and Hull-White are the standard tools for modelling the short rate — the mean reversion ensures that interest rates stay within realistic bounds and the yield curve can be fitted exactly.

label: mathematical basis for
description: The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck mean-reverting process provides the mathematical foundation for mean reversion strategies — it models the speed and strength of the pull back towards equilibrium that the strategy exploits.

label: tested by
description: The ADF test's null hypothesis is a unit root (random walk), while the alternative is stationarity — rejecting the null is the standard way to statistically confirm mean reversion in a time series.

label: modifies
description: Mean reversion adds a restoring drift term κ(θ − X) to the Brownian motion framework — without it, Brownian motion drifts without bound, which is unsuitable for quantities like interest rates.

label: state-dependent
description: In the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process the drift coefficient is κ(θ − X), which depends on the current state — when X is above θ the drift is negative, pulling the process back down, and vice versa.

label: implies existence of
description: Mean-reverting processes (like Ornstein-Uhlenbeck) possess a stationary distribution — the Fokker-Planck steady-state equation provides the analytical form of this long-run density.

label: shapes
description: Mean reversion in volatility determines the shape of the term structure — fast mean reversion produces a steeply sloped or inverted curve as short-term vol deviates from the long-run level.

label: improves on
description: The Milstein method adds a correction term — half b·b'(ΔW² − δt) — to the Euler-Maruyama discretisation, raising strong convergence from O(sqrt(δt)) to O(δt) and reducing bias in path-dependent Monte Carlo estimates.

label: discretises
description: The Milstein method is a numerical scheme for solving SDEs by time-stepping — it discretises the continuous SDE dS = a dt + b dW into a recurrence relation that converges to the true solution as the step size δt tends to zero.

label: generalises
description: Applying the multi-asset Ito s lemma to a portfolio of correlated assets leads to the multi-dimensional Black-Scholes PDE, which prices basket and spread options.

label: extends
description: The multi-asset Ito s lemma extends the single-asset version by adding cross terms dS1 dS2 that capture the interaction between correlated assets.

label: validates
description: The Novikov condition guarantees that an exponential local martingale is a true martingale with unit expectation — ensuring the change-of-measure density defines a proper probability measure.

label: prerequisite for
description: Girsanov’s theorem requires the Novikov condition to guarantee that the Radon–Nikodým derivative is a true martingale — without it, the change of measure may produce a defective probability measure.

label: used in
description: The American option price is the supremum over all stopping times of the discounted expected payoff — the optional stopping theorem ensures this optimisation is well-defined and that the Snell envelope gives the correct price.

label: applied to
description: Applying the optional stopping theorem to Brownian motion at barrier hitting times gives the probabilities used in pricing knock-out and knock-in barrier options.

label: extends
description: The optional stopping theorem extends the martingale property from deterministic times to random stopping times — under regularity conditions, E[M_τ] = E[M_0] even when τ is chosen based on the process.

label: solved by
description: The Fokker–Planck equation for the OU process has an exact Gaussian solution whose mean decays exponentially to μ and whose variance converges to σ²/2θ — this stationary distribution is used when calibrating mean-reverting interest rate models to historical data.

label: canonical model of
description: The OU process is the canonical continuous-time model of mean reversion — it is the only linear SDE with a stationary distribution, making it the standard building block for interest rate and commodity price models.

label: instance of
description: The OU process is defined by a linear SDE with constant coefficients — its analytical tractability (exact Gaussian solution) makes it the archetypal example for illustrating SDE solution techniques.

label: property of
description: Quadratic variation [W]_t = t is a defining property of Brownian motion — it quantifies the path roughness that distinguishes stochastic from deterministic processes.

label: continuous limit of
description: The discrete random walk has quadratic variation equal to the number of steps (each squared increment is 1) — rescaling to continuous time gives the Brownian motion result [W]_t = t.

label: implies
description: The rule dW² = dt is the infinitesimal form of quadratic variation — [W]_t = t in integral form becomes dW² = dt in differential form.

label: foundation of
description: Non-zero quadratic variation is the reason the second-order Taylor term survives in stochastic calculus — this directly produces the Itô correction term ½σ²f′′ dt in Itô’s lemma.

label: constructed as
description: In continuous time, the Radon–Nikodým derivative is constructed as an exponential martingale — ensuring positivity and unit expectation so that the new measure is a valid probability measure.

label: used by
description: Girsanov’s theorem specifies the Radon–Nikodým derivative that effects the change of measure, transforming a drifting process into a standard Brownian motion under the new measure.

label: explains
description: Volatility clustering — the tendency for large moves to follow large moves — arises naturally in regime-switching models because the high-volatility regime persists for multiple periods before transitioning back to the calm regime.

label: generalises
description: Regime-switching models generalise GBM by allowing the drift and volatility parameters to change with an unobserved Markov state, whereas GBM assumes constant parameters throughout.

label: alternative to
description: Both regime-switching and jump-diffusion models address the failure of GBM to capture fat tails and abrupt market moves, but they do so differently — regime switching changes the parameter set discretely, while jump diffusion adds Poisson-driven discontinuities to the price path.

label: can incorporate
description: Regime-switching models can assign different mean-reversion speeds to each regime, capturing the observation that markets may trend in calm periods but revert in volatile periods.

label: prerequisite for
description: The Fokker–Planck equation is written directly in terms of the drift A and diffusion B from standard form — you must identify these coefficients before you can write down the transition density PDE.

label: classifies
description: Writing Vasicek and CIR in standard form reveals that they share the same drift structure A = ν − μS but differ in diffusion (constant vs sqrt(S)) — standard form makes model comparison systematic.

label: organises
description: Standard form dG = A dt + B dW_t provides a systematic way to identify drift and diffusion in any SDE, regardless of how the equation is originally written.

label: foundation of
description: The SDE dS = μS dt + σS dX is the starting point for deriving the Black-Scholes PDE — applying Itô's Lemma and the no-arbitrage condition to this equation produces the pricing formula.

label: decomposed into
description: The SDE dS = μS dt + σS dW splits into a drift term and a diffusion term — the diffusion coefficient b(S) = σS is the function that controls the size of the random component.

label: parameterised by
description: The SDE contains exactly two parameters — drift μ and volatility σ — which fully specify the probabilistic behaviour of the asset price.

label: decomposed into
description: The SDE dS = μS dt + σS dW splits into a drift term and a diffusion term — the drift coefficient a(S) = μS is the function that controls the expected rate of change.

label: integrates against
description: Stochastic integrals are defined as integrals with respect to Brownian motion dW — unlike Riemann integrals, the integrator is a random process with non-zero quadratic variation.

label: integral form of
description: Stochastic integrals are Itô’s lemma rearranged into integral form — the dW integral equals the total change in F minus the dt integral, providing a way to evaluate integrals against Brownian motion.

label: produces
description: Itô integrals ∫ g(W) dW are martingales with zero expectation — this property is fundamental to risk-neutral pricing where hedging strategies are expressed as stochastic integrals.

label: foundation of
description: The Snell envelope is defined as the smallest supermartingale dominating the discounted payoff process — the supermartingale property encodes that the American option’s expected discounted value can only decrease as the exercise right is consumed.

label: generalises
description: Submartingales (non-negative drift) and supermartingales (non-positive drift) generalise martingales (zero drift) — a martingale is the special case that is simultaneously both.

label: extends
description: The zero-drift condition identifies martingales (drift = 0); submartingales and supermartingales extend this to processes with positive or negative drift respectively.

label: enables
description: The tower property guarantees that multi-step risk-neutral pricing is self-consistent — pricing to maturity via an intermediate date gives the same answer as pricing directly, which underpins backward induction in binomial trees and American option pricing.

label: underpins
description: The tower property proves that a martingale has constant unconditional expectation — by iterating the conditional expectation, E[M_t] = E[E[M_t | F_s]] = E[M_s] for all s < t.

label: uses
description: For processes with constant coefficients (e.g. Brownian motion, Ornstein-Uhlenbeck), the transition density takes the form of a normal distribution whose mean and variance depend on the current state and time interval.

label: specialises
description: The transition probability density is a conditional probability density function — it gives the PDF of the future state conditioned on the current state, inheriting all PDF properties including normalisation.

label: simplest case
description: Brownian motion provides the simplest non-trivial transition density — a Gaussian whose variance grows linearly with the time interval, serving as the building block for more complex process densities.

label: solved by
description: The Fokker-Planck equation is the forward PDE that the transition probability density satisfies — solving it with a delta-function initial condition yields the transition density explicitly.

label: converges to
description: As the step size and time step shrink to zero with the ratio 2α(δy)²/δt held constant, the trinomial random walk converges in distribution to Brownian motion — the three-outcome structure provides a richer discrete approximation than the binomial walk.

label: determines
description: The diffusion coefficient D emerges from the trinomial walk through the scaling relation D = 2α(δy)²/δt — choosing α, δy, and δt to satisfy this constraint ensures the discrete variance matches the continuous diffusion rate.

label: generalises
description: The trinomial walk extends the two-outcome discrete random walk by adding a stationary option at each step — this extra degree of freedom allows independent control of the step size and the transition probability, improving lattice convergence.

label: derives
description: Taylor-expanding the trinomial transition probabilities and taking the continuum limit yields the Fokker-Planck equation ∂p/∂t = (D/2)∂²p/∂y² — the trinomial framework is the most direct discrete route to this PDE.

label: driven by
description: The Vasicek model adds mean-reverting drift to Brownian motion — the stochastic term σ dW provides the randomness while the drift κ(θ − r) pulls the rate toward equilibrium.

label: priced via
description: Bond prices in the Vasicek model are derived by changing to the risk-neutral measure via Girsanov’s theorem, replacing the physical drift with a risk-adjusted drift and computing discounted expectations.

label: satisfies
description: The Vasicek model is a Markov process because its drift depends only on the current rate r_t, not on its history — enabling PDE-based bond pricing where the price depends only on (r, t).

label: implies
description: The volatility drag correction shows that log-prices drift at mu - sigma^2/2, making log S normally distributed and hence S lognormally distributed.

label: explains
description: Volatility drag explains why the median GBM outcome grows at mu - sigma^2/2 rather than mu — higher volatility lowers the compound growth rate even though the expected value still grows at mu.

label: foundation of
description: The zero-drift condition is the mathematical mechanism behind risk-neutral pricing — by finding the measure under which discounted prices are martingales (zero drift), derivatives can be priced as expected values.

label: constructs
description: The zero-drift condition finds a transformation g(S) that eliminates the drift, making g(S) a martingale — a process where today s value is the best forecast of the future.

label: extends
description: The correlation smile extends the concept of correlation from a single number to a strike-dependent function, revealing that the market prices in higher co-movement during sell-offs.

label: analogous to
description: The correlation smile is the correlation-space analogue of the volatility smile — just as implied vol varies with strike, implied correlation varies with the moneyness of index options.

label: links index and single-stock
description: The correlation smile emerges from the difference between the index volatility surface and the average single-stock volatility surface — it quantifies how diversification changes across moneyness levels.

label: extends
description: The Heston model generalises Black-Scholes by replacing the constant-volatility assumption with a stochastic variance process — Black-Scholes is recovered as the special case where the vol of vol is zero. Black–Scholes is recovered as the special case of the Heston model when the vol-of-vol σ_v → 0 and the initial variance equals θ — Heston nests BS as a degenerate limit, providing a consistency check on the more general framework.

label: violates
description: The Heston model introduces stochastic volatility as a second source of randomness that cannot be hedged with the stock alone, making the market incomplete and requiring an additional instrument or a market price of volatility risk.

label: priced via
description: Although the Heston model has a semi-analytical solution for European options, exotic and path-dependent derivatives under Heston are typically priced by Monte Carlo simulation of the two correlated SDEs.

label: generalises
description: GBM assumes constant volatility, while the Heston model replaces this with a mean-reverting stochastic variance process — the asset price SDE is augmented with a second coupled SDE for the variance.

label: uses
description: The Heston model's variance process is a mean-reverting square-root diffusion (CIR process) — the speed of mean reversion κ and long-run variance θ control how quickly volatility returns to its equilibrium after a shock.

label: uses
description: The Heston model involves two correlated stochastic differential equations (price and variance) — applying multi-asset Itô's lemma is required to derive the Heston PDE from the coupled SDEs.

label: explains
description: The Heston model's stochastic volatility and negative correlation between returns and variance reproduce the implied volatility smile and skew that Black-Scholes' flat volatility assumption cannot capture.

label: generates
description: The Heston model generates a volatility surface from its five parameters — calibrating Heston to market data means matching its implied surface to the observed one.

label: calibrated to
description: Heston's mean-reversion speed and vol-of-vol parameters are calibrated to match the observed volatility term structure — the term structure pins down the dynamics of variance over time.

label: quantified by
description: Vega translates a change in implied volatility into a change in option price — it is the conversion factor that links the volatility market to the options market.

label: reveals
description: The volatility smile and skew observed in implied volatility across strikes expose the failure of Black-Scholes' constant-volatility assumption — the market prices in fat tails and jump risk that the model ignores.

label: compared in
description: Implied volatility is one half of the implied-vs-realised comparison — the forward-looking market expectation whose accuracy is judged against subsequent realised moves.

label: determines P&L of
description: The profit or loss from a delta-hedged option position depends on whether realised volatility exceeds or falls short of the implied volatility at which the option was traded.

label: quantified by
description: The implied-vs-realised spread is the observable manifestation of the variance risk premium — the VRP formalises and measures this spread as a tradeable risk factor.

label: settled by
description: Variance swaps pay the difference between realised and implied variance, making them the purest instrument for monetising the implied-vs-realised spread without path-dependent Greeks.

label: generalises
description: Local volatility generalises Black-Scholes by replacing the constant volatility parameter with a function sigma(S,t), reducing to Black-Scholes when the local vol surface is flat.

label: complemented by
description: Local volatility is complemented by the Heston stochastic volatility model — local vol gets the smile right today but has wrong dynamics, while Heston captures volatility dynamics but may not fit the smile exactly.

label: calibrates to
description: The local volatility model is uniquely determined by calibration to the observed volatility smile — the Dupire equation extracts the local vol surface directly from market option prices.

label: calibrated to
description: The Dupire local volatility function is uniquely determined by the volatility surface — it is the deterministic volatility field that exactly reproduces every point on the surface.

label: feeds into
description: Volatility scaling uses realised volatility estimates to normalise position sizes or risk exposures, ensuring that portfolio risk remains constant as market conditions change.

label: estimated by
description: EWMA is a weighted estimator of realised volatility that gives greater weight to recent returns, providing a more responsive measure than the simple rolling-window standard deviation.

label: modelled by
description: GARCH models the conditional dynamics of realised volatility, capturing clustering and mean reversion — features that a simple rolling-window estimator cannot represent.

label: compared in
description: Realised volatility is the backward-looking half of the implied-vs-realised comparison — the actual price variability that determines whether an option buyer or seller profits on a delta-hedged basis.

label: calibrated to
description: SABR is calibrated to the swaption volatility cube — each expiry-tenor pair has its own set of SABR parameters fitted to the market smile across strikes.

label: alternative to
description: SABR and Heston are the two dominant stochastic volatility models — SABR is preferred on rates/FX desks for its analytical tractability, while Heston is more common for equities.

label: preferred over
description: SABR is preferred over local volatility on rates desks because it produces more realistic smile dynamics — the smile moves with the forward in a way that matches empirical behaviour and produces more stable hedges.

label: models
description: SABR is the standard model for fitting and interpolating the volatility smile on rates and FX desks — its four parameters capture the level, skew, and curvature of the smile.

label: parameterises
description: SABR provides a parsimonious parameterisation of the volatility surface for interest rate and FX options, allowing smooth interpolation and extrapolation from a few calibrated parameters.

label: generalises
description: Stochastic volatility models generalise Black-Scholes by replacing its constant-volatility assumption with a random volatility process, enabling the model to reproduce the volatility smile that Black-Scholes cannot.

label: parent class of
description: The Heston model is the most widely used specific instance of the stochastic volatility framework, choosing a CIR-type mean-reverting square-root process for the variance dynamics.

label: parent class of
description: SABR is a stochastic volatility model tailored to interest rate and FX markets, where the forward price and its volatility both follow correlated diffusions with a CEV-type structure.

label: explains
description: Stochastic volatility models were developed specifically to explain the volatility smile — the correlation between asset returns and volatility shocks generates the strike-dependent implied volatility pattern that constant-volatility models cannot produce.

label: inflates
description: The variance risk premium is the reason implied volatility systematically exceeds realised volatility — the premium is embedded in option prices as an insurance charge.

label: captured via
description: Variance swaps are the purest instrument for harvesting the variance risk premium — the difference between the swap's fixed strike (implied) and its floating leg (realised) directly isolates the VRP.

label: proxied by
description: The VIX squared minus subsequent realised variance is the standard empirical proxy for the variance risk premium, making the VIX the most widely watched VRP indicator.

label: priced under
description: The Heston model provides a natural framework for pricing variance swaps, as its characteristic function yields a closed-form expression for the expected realised variance.

label: priced from surface of
description: The fair strike of a variance swap is determined by integrating over the entire implied volatility surface — it depends on option prices at all strikes, not just at-the-money volatility.

label: encodes information from
description: The variance swap strike encodes information from the entire volatility smile — it is a weighted integral of implied variances across all strikes, making it sensitive to skew and kurtosis.

label: embeds
description: The VIX futures term structure embeds the variance risk premium — contango reflects the cost of insuring against volatility spikes, and the roll yield earned by short-vol positions is a manifestation of this premium.

label: extends
description: VIX futures extend the spot VIX into a forward curve, showing the market's expectation of implied volatility at future dates plus a risk premium for bearing volatility risk.

label: parallels
description: The VIX futures term structure parallels the implied volatility term structure but for forward-starting implied vol — both curves respond to the same mean-reversion dynamics and risk premia.

label: index of
description: The VIX is a model-free index of implied volatility that incorporates information from all listed SPX option strikes, not just the at-the-money implied vol.

label: derived from same formula as
description: The VIX calculation is mathematically equivalent to the fair strike of a 30-day variance swap on the S&P 500 — both use the same replicating portfolio of out-of-the-money options.

label: aggregates
description: The VIX aggregates the entire strike dimension of the 30-day volatility surface into a single number, weighting each option by 1/K² to capture the full smile.

label: shaped by
description: The cone's characteristic funnel shape arises from the square-root-of-time rule — short-window realised vol is more dispersed than long-window vol because extreme readings are more likely over shorter periods.

label: visualises
description: Overlaying implied volatility on the realised volatility cone directly visualises the implied-vs-realised comparison across all maturities, making it a practical tool for identifying trading opportunities.

label: contextualises
description: The volatility cone plots the historical distribution of realised volatility at each measurement window, providing the benchmark against which current implied volatility is judged rich or cheap.

label: exposes limitation of
description: The existence of the smile directly contradicts Black-Scholes' constant-volatility assumption — if the model were exact, implied volatility would be flat across all strikes.

label: explained by
description: Vanna captures how Delta shifts when volatility changes — the correlation between spot and volatility moves (the leverage effect) is a primary driver of the skew component of the volatility smile.

label: complicates
description: The volatility smile means implied volatility varies by strike, so Vega exposure differs across the strike spectrum — a portfolio can be Vega-neutral in aggregate but still exposed to skew and curvature moves.

label: explained by
description: OTM options have positive Volga, meaning they benefit from volatility convexity — the premium traders pay for this convexity is a key driver of the volatility smile.

label: evidences
description: The volatility smile is empirical proof that real return distributions have fatter tails and asymmetry compared to the lognormal — it is one of the most visible model limitations in practice.

label: reproduced by
description: The Heston model's stochastic volatility and correlation between returns and variance generate an implied volatility smile from first principles, providing a theoretically grounded alternative to strike-by-strike fitting.

label: maps across strikes
description: The volatility smile is the graph of implied volatility as a function of strike price — each point on the smile is an implied volatility extracted from a single option's market price via the Black-Scholes formula.

label: maps
description: The volatility surface is the complete map of implied volatility across all strikes and maturities, extending a single implied vol quote into a continuous function.

label: contains
description: The volatility smile is a single maturity slice of the volatility surface — the surface generalises the smile by adding the term structure dimension.

label: settles against
description: A volatility swap's floating leg is the realised volatility of the underlying over the contract period, making it a direct bet on the level of future realised vol.

label: compared with
description: Volatility swaps pay linearly in realised vol while variance swaps pay quadratically — the convexity adjustment between the two captures the vol-of-vol and is a key concept in volatility derivatives.

label: organises
description: The term structure organises ATM implied volatility by maturity, revealing whether the market expects current volatility conditions to persist or mean-revert.

label: is a dimension of
description: The volatility term structure is the time axis of the volatility surface — at each maturity the surface has a smile across strikes, and the ATM slice across maturities forms the term structure.
